data.title: Trump’s TikTok deal sets a very dangerous precedent
data.description:
With his glamorous first lady at his side, the president boasted how an “economically free, politically sovereign” nation was emerging from the chaos and corruption of the recent past.

Top of his achievements so far, he boasted, was taming an irresponsible media.

This wasn’t Donald Trump, however, but Juan Perón, the president of Argentina, speaking in 1950.

Nicknamed El Primer Trabajador – the “first worker” – Perón rejected global trade and championed the working-class man, like Trump.

Perón also closed 45 newspapers hostile to his administration, suing two of the largest for libel. Like Peron, Trump has also launched lawsuits against the New York Times and the Wall Street Journal for publishing defamatory articles.

As a result, the administration now looks more Peronist than ever, particularly after deposing another critical TV show host.

After Stephen Colbert’s show was pulled in July, Jimmy Kimmel was suspended by ABC last week after Brendan Carr, the chairman of the Federal Communications Commission (FCC), threatened to revoke the broadcaster’s license in the wake of comments by the late night talk show host about the shooting of Charlie Kirk.

Conservative torch-bearer and US senator Ted Cruz later compared the threat to “mafioso” tactics.

But that’s not even the biggest Trump media story of last week. Particularly as only a handful of 45 to 60-year-olds watch late-night US TV: barely over a million in all, in Kimmel’s case, while Colbert’s show was reportedly losing $40m (£29m) a year.

By contrast, something far more valuable is about to fall into the lap of Team Trump: the biggest and most-watched media platform in the world, TikTok.

The proposed deal to solidify American ownership of the Chinese app confirms the emergence of the Ellisons, the dynasty headed by Oracle founder Larry Ellison, as the most powerful media family in the world and one very close to the president.

The family already owns Paramount and CBS, whilst Ellison’s Oracle is now set to also control TikTok as part of a US consortium after Donald Trump struck a deal with China.

But there are two really remarkable aspects to this story which merit more attention.

One is that this is a straightforward asset seizure, which has not gone unnoticed by American executives with investments in China and the BRICS. The other is Silicon Valley’s role in the story.

The internet companies have a dirty secret: they hate competition and are not very good at it.

If you can’t beat them, buy them, and if you can’t buy them, ban them.

Five years ago, TikTok’s owner ByteDance was trouncing Silicon Valley at its own game, with a wildly addictive new video service.

It had effectively created a new medium. TikTok’s algorithm was so compelling that it was being compared to crack cocaine, and users loved it for another reason too: it didn’t splatter them with ads.

TikTok is more like QVC, a shop window, with an affiliate marketing model. It now has two billion users.

Knowing the Silicon Valley executive cadre well, I find the most compelling theory for TikTok’s story to be this.

Global success and the absence of competition had rendered the social media platforms lazy.

They were enmeshed in “technical debt”, which means layers of ageing computer code that are difficult to maintain but also difficult to replace. And they were welded to a model based on advertising.

Think how many PhDs have walked through the doors of Facebook and Google to devote their planet sized brains to arcane matters such as fine-tuning cost-per-click algorithms, only to discover that the punters would rather not see those ads at all.

Competition means consumers can vote with their feet, and off they marched, en masse, to TikTok.

“We observed that our growth slowed down dramatically,” Zuckerberg said earlier this year regarding the rise of TikTok. So what to do?

The usual answer to an upstart is to make it an offer it can’t refuse. But there was a problem. As ByteDance was a Beijing-based company registered in the Cayman Islands, buying TikTok wasn’t a ready option.

Time to break the glass and pull the emergency cord called national security.

“A decade ago, almost all the major internet platforms were American,” Mark Zuckerberg told an audience in 2019. “Today, six of the top 10 are Chinese.”

The spooks themselves were more ambivalent, however.

They had much bigger fish to fry, such as Huawei, which had embedded itself in much of our critical telecommunications infrastructure.

They let this be known, via the New York Times. If TikTok were a national security threat, why let it continue in any form at all?

Banning TikTok became Plan A. Trump passed an executive order in 2019 to shut it down, and this came into force just as he was being sworn in for the second time.

Shutting down a superior rival is good, but acquiring its secret sauce is even better, and so it has landed, fairly intact, in the hands of new American owners.

This is just the sort of deal Trump likes: his friends get something, the addicts get their digital drug back, and he looks like a hero. Yet it’s all a little too neat. There are grave dangers for US Inc in seizing competitors’ business assets.

Silicon Valley is already playing the national security card on AI, having built the wrong kind. Leaning on the government to crush competition by seizing assets sets a dangerous precedent.

American business has $150bn of assets in BRICS countries and around a trillion dollars worth in China. They may conclude that what’s sauce for the goose is sauce for the gander.

data.title: Financial Times writes off 90pc of investment in European tech coverage
data.description:
The Financial Times has written off the majority of an investment into covering European start-ups amid a downturn in tech funding on the Continent.

The FT said in recently filed accounts that it spent £614,000 on raising its stake in Sifted, a news site aimed at tech entrepreneurs and investors, from 14.4pc to 17.5pc in June 2024.

However, accounts for the same year show it cut the value of its investment by £550,000 just months later. It means almost 90pc of the money pumped into Sifted was effectively wiped out within months.

Touted as a “critical friend of the European start-up ecosystem”, Sifted was launched by John Thornhill, FT’s innovation editor, in 2019 and was backed financially by the newspaper.

Launched as a free news service, Sifted put a paywall in place in 2021. Last year it claimed to be reaching an audience of 120,000 with its daily newsletters and said subscribers to its “pro” offering were up 32pc year on year.

The latest accounts for Sifted show it reduced its headcount from 56 staff members in 2023 to 47 in 2024.

The FT said in its accounts: “Following the completion of the annual impairment review exercise, management concluded that the carrying value of Sifted (EU) Limited could not be supported and the decision was made to record an impairment charge of £555,000.”

Last month, the FT confirmed it would close down The Next Web, an annual tech conference held in Amsterdam, following a strategic review.

The FT had taken a stake in The Next Web, which also operates a news site and was founded in 2006, six years ago as part of a push to “deepen the FT’s reach into the European technology community”.

Digital publishers have been grappling with intense pressure on web traffic driven by changes in search from artificial intelligence. Many are attempting to shift to subscription-based business models.

Other media organisations have also been paring back their coverage of European technology businesses.

TechCrunch, the US news brand, laid off its 10-strong European editorial team in May. Its publisher, private equity firm Regent, insisted it was not “retreating from Europe”.

European technology businesses, meanwhile, have seen a downturn in investment from a high point in 2021 driven by low interest rates. Funding into European start-ups was around $45bn (£33bn) in 2024, according to a report from venture capital firm Atomico, down from more than $100bn during the pandemic.

The FT’s overall revenues rose by 2.5pc in 2024 to £454.6m, while pre-tax profit declined marginally to £3.89m, accounts showed.

Subscribers to its print and digital coverage rose to record levels over the year. Print and digital subscribers increased by 6pc to 1.5 million, with digital-only subscriptions rising 6pc to 1.35 million.

However, it posted a slump in advertising revenues, which fell from £151.6m to £146m.

The FT was approached for comment.

data.title: Yours for just £280: An ‘ethically made’ Guardian jumper
data.description:
The Guardian is selling jumpers for £281 after it reported a loss of almost £30m in the most recent financial year.

Lingua Franca, an American luxury clothing brand, has announced the collaboration with the newspaper to highlight the “importance of fiercely independent journalism”.

The jumpers, listed at $380 (£281), incorporate five Guardian phrases embroidered into the clothing, such as “facts are sacred” and “for facts’ sake”.

Other expressions featured on the sweaters include “fiercely independent”, “the whole picture”, and simply “The Guardian”.

It comes after the Guardian’s holding company reported a £28.8m loss after tax for the year ending in March, according to accounts filed at Companies House.

In a separate annual report, the publication said it generated £275.9m in revenues, an £18.1m increase from £257.8m a year earlier, and that its cash outflow fell from £36.5m to £24.3m. It also reported an increase in “recurring supporters” to 1.3m people.

The newspaper enjoys the backing of the £1.2bn Scott Trust endowment fund, which is intended to support it “in perpetuity”.

In its annual report, Ole Jacob Sunde, the chairman of the Scott Trust, said the Guardian’s losses remained a “substantial number”, but that they were driven by “deliberate investment in editorial capabilities and technology”.

The pressure on the newspaper’s finances comes despite efforts to cut the group’s losses in recent years. Last year, it launched a voluntary redundancy programme for its journalists amid pressure on print advertising and web traffic.

It also sold its loss-making Observer newspaper to Tortoise Media late last year, despite anger from some staff over the deal.

Clothing ‘in alignment with Guardian values’

The jumpers – available in Lingua Franca’s New York City store in October and online – come in up to eight different colours. Its “fiercely independent” children’s crewneck, priced at $125 (£93), has already sold out.

The publication said the Lingua Franca clothing collection is “in alignment with the Guardian’s values”, made with a “focus on natural materials, sustainable production and ethical labour”.

The Lingua Franca website said: “The Guardian is fiercely independent. Nobody influences their journalism.

“They believe that facts are sacred and deliver truth, humanity, and global insight to bring people the whole picture, for free.”

A fifth of the profits from the sales will go to the newspaper to support its journalism, with “the mission to advance and inform public discourse and citizen participation around the most pressing issues of our time”.

It follows the release of the Guardian’s month-long US marketing campaign on Monday, which included tag lines such as, “Uncover what’s truly going on” and “Get wind of climate’s biggest winners and losers”, alongside pictures of Donald Trump, wind turbines and severe weather conditions.

Sara Badler, the chief advertising officer in North America for the Guardian, said: “We are so excited about the Guardian’s collaboration with Lingua Franca, demonstrating how brands can reach audiences in ways that are not only impactful but also authentic and lasting.

“It’s a natural extension of our US brand campaign, ‘The whole picture’, featuring the breadth of the Guardian’s global and independent journalism, which audiences have loyally turned to for more than 200 years.”

data.title: Meghan offered show on Magic Radio
data.description:
Magic Radio has offered to make the Duchess of Sussex a disc jockey after she revealed she missed listening to it.

The Duchess, who moved to California in June 2020, said in her Netflix series, With Love, Meghan, that Magic was “one of the things I miss most about the UK”.

Paul Sylvester, content director of Magic, told a RadioCentre conference on Tuesday: “We had conversations with the Sussex team and if the Duchess would like to add radio presenter to her CV there is an offer on the table.”

Magic said it had “been in with her team and offered her a show, if she ever wanted one”.

It was not revealed how much the Duchess was offered, but UK radio presenter salaries are unlikely to be on the same level as her previous media deals.

The Duke and Duchess were reportedly given a $100m (£74m) five-year deal by Netflix in 2020. They also secured a short-lived deal with Spotify, said to be worth £15.5m.

Owned by Bauer Media, Magic plays music released from the 1980s onwards. It is one of the most popular radio networks in the UK, with 3.6 million weekly listeners, according to Rajar.

The offer from Magic comes despite the Duke and Duchess of Sussex’s struggles to establish themselves in the media world.

Their Spotify-commissioned podcast, Archetypes, was axed after just one series, with critics claiming that that the Duchess’s interview style was “cringe-worthy” and involved talking too much about herself.

Bill Simmons, a Spotify boss, lashed out at the pair as “f---ing grifters” after the end of the podcast deal.

The official line from the streaming giant and the Sussexes’ Archewell charity was that they were “proud of the series we made together”.

In April, the Duchess took another stab at podcasting with an eight-part series, Confessions of a Female Founder. The show was widely panned, with reviewers claiming it contained “surface-level waffle” and was “stomach-turning”.

It quickly dropped out of the Spotify 100 list of top podcasts on both sides of the Atlantic.

The Duke and Duchess have now signed a new multi-year deal with Netflix, which is said to be less extensive and less well-paid than the original agreement.

The Sussexes were approached for comment.

data.title: Thames Water’s rescue plans risks setting a dangerous precedent
data.description:
The latest version of a creditor-led rescue plan for Thames Water ticks multiple boxes. One can see why the company, ministers and the industry regulator might be minded to give it the green light in the coming weeks.

The pressure to hammer out a deal is intense. The threat of a multibillion-pound bill landing on Rachel Reeves’s desk before her Budget in November is concentrating Whitehall minds.

Thames is burning cash rapidly. A separate announcement from the company revealed that in August it called on the remaining £628m of a £1.5bn emergency loan.

It is further worrying evidence of the extent to which an organisation providing critical services to 13 million people continues to live hand-to-mouth after years of reckless ownership.

With the clock ticking, the “London and Valley” consortium of lenders has come up with a decent upgrade on the somewhat vague proposal published a month ago.

Then, the prospective new owners declared their undying commitment to “a new transparent and collaborative relationship with regulators”, before outlining a plan lacking in details.

The highlights of their latest attempt include a more generous portion of equity and a bigger debt write-off than previously envisaged. A commitment to not pay dividends for five years, along with the ambition of eventually returning Thames to the stock market are also welcome measures.

Yet all the hard work that has gone into answering many of the concerns of regulators, Government and long-suffering customers risks being overshadowed by what amounts to a fresh attempt to wriggle out of fines.

Having sensibly given up on trying to dodge outstanding penalties for pumping sewage into waterways, Thames’s wannabe owners are now trying to inoculate themselves against future punishment.

“The current regulatory framework for the water industry does not allow it to successfully turnaround its business,” London and Valley says.

The consortium won’t take “no” for an answer, it seems.

Steve Reed, the former environment secretary, told parliament in June that Thames must meet its statutory obligations, after being asked about possible “regulatory easements”.

But the very idea that an industry that treats Britain’s rivers as its dumping ground should face softer environmental targets – or “stretching but achievable incentives” in the consortium’s words – won’t wash.

It’s hard to square this with the consortium’s warm words.

Mike McTighe, the proposed future chairman of Thames – he is also chairman of the company behind The Telegraph – talks about “rebuilding public trust”.

The creditors themselves admit that Thames has presided over “a cycle of underinvestment and underperformance that has driven trust in Thames Water to an all-time low.”

Few would argue with that assessment. Yet any agreement that makes it easier for the company to pollute with greater impunity is an improbable basis for the restoration of Thames’s relationship with the public.

Consider that in an industry that has vandalised the environment, this is a chronic offender.

Thames’s record is getting worse, not better. Some 50pc more raw sewage was discharged into Britain’s waterways from Thames’s ageing system last year, compared with the previous 12 months.

In May, Thames Water was fined £123m by Ofwat – the biggest ever fine imposed by the regulator.

From a purely financial perspective, the creditors’ plea for clemency is understandable.

They expect future fines of more than £1bn if current regulations are applied and argue that something has to give if Thames is to receive the investment it needs.

This financial hole was of Thames’s own making, however. Its creditors bankrolled it as it hollowed itself out. To turn around now and demand that the rules are changed to make pumping effluent into rivers a less costly business is an affront to justice and risks setting a horrible precedent.

Perhaps even now, after months and months of negotiations, the hedge funds that swooped in to buy up debt on the cheap when Thames was on the brink, still don’t understand the extent of public and political anger.

Customer ire stretches to the whole gamut of misdeeds – rising bills, poor service, undeserved dividends and excessive executive pay.

But it is the epidemic of pollution that has attracted the most fury. That the creditors have chosen to attack on that front seems “courageous”, as they say in the Civil Service.

Indeed, I am willing to bet that the majority of people want to see stiffer fines, not softer ones, particularly if the current set-up has failed to act as a deterrent. Weaker rules would surely lead to more pollution, not less.

Meanwhile, the knock-on effect of granting Thames an easier ride would be that every other water company in Britain would be entitled to expect the same exemptions on the basis of fairness.

A reminder from the creditors that their offer is the only credible one left after private equity firm KKR abandoned its bid looks designed to focus minds. Ditto talks of how the plan “will avoid special administration”, and ensure that “Thames Water’s turnaround will not fall to the UK taxpayer”.

Decency, however, demands that Ofwat and the Government don’t allow themselves to be pushed around by a bunch of opportunistic hedge funds.

data.title: BT boss attacks ‘government-inflicted costs’
data.description:
The chief executive of BT has attacked “government-inflicted costs” for holding back British businesses.

Allison Kirkby said the telecoms giant was lagging behind its European rivals because of punishing costs heaped on the group by the Labour Government.

She said taxes, levies and red tape meant its spending bill was significantly higher than other companies on the continent.

“We pay in business rates, energy levies and other costs associated with regulation and compliance 10 times the amount our peers pay in countries like Germany and the Netherlands,” she told industry conference Connected Britain on Wednesday.

Ms Kirkby, who was appointed chief executive last year, added that BT was “already at peak government-inflicted costs”.

BT previously admitted the 2024 Budget had cost the company an additional £100m, largely because of an increase in employer National Insurance contributions.

Alongside the rise in labour costs, telecoms companies are also grappling with the UK’s high electricity costs. 

British companies pay the highest electricity prices of anywhere in the developed world, according to government figures.

Electricity in the UK is now about 50pc more expensive than in Germany or France and four times more expensive than in the US.

Ms Kirkby said Rachel Reeves’s Budget on November 26 could be “very difficult” for the Chancellor.

Economists have said Ms Reeves must fill an estimated £20bn shortfall in the public finances to rescue her fiscal plans.

Ms Kirkby, who is a government adviser on trade, called for greater fiscal stability and said the UK is “a sluggish, high inflation market at the moment and we absolutely, as a country, need to get back to growth”.

Business lobby groups and companies have warned the Chancellor to avoid raising taxes on businesses in the upcoming Budget, amid a mounting business backlash against the Government over anti-growth policies.

On Wednesday, oil and gas producer EnQuest accused Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, of creating a “self-inflicted wound” if he allowed the decline of the North Sea to accelerate.

Amjad Bseisu, the company’s chief executive, said there had been a 90pc reduction in investment in the North Sea basin over the last decade to about £2bn last year – forcing Britain to go from being a gas exporter to importing half its stocks of the fossil fuel.

The Energy Secretary is poised to reverse Labour’s ban on new oil and gas drilling amid warnings from union bosses that net zero risks crushing British industry.

Mr Bseisu suggested the Chancellor could also help address the gaping hole in the public finances by cutting windfall taxes on the oil and gas sector.

He urged the Chancellor to make a “dramatic change” to the energy profits levy, which imposes a 78pc tax on North Sea profits.

“Even 10 years ago the revenue base was about £14bn which was significantly higher than it is today. The key is growth. The key is having a fiscal regime that actually fosters growth,” he said.

data.title: Hedge funds bet against Scottish turbine company as Trump attacks ‘windmills’
data.description:
A major North Sea energy supplier has become the most shorted company on the UK stock market amid attacks from Donald Trump on offshore wind turbines.

Ashtead Technology, which rents equipment to offshore oil, gas and wind projects, has 7.5pc of its shares in the hands of hedge funds, which have sold them short to profit from an expected share price decline.

Hedge funds short companies when they think their share price will drop, selling shares and then buying them back at a lower price to profit from the drop in value.

According to data from the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA), funds including Ken Griffin’s Citadel, Acadian Asset Management and GLG Partners have shorted Ashtead as they bet on a further share price swoon in future.

Shares have already fallen 39pc this year amid bleak prospects for the North Sea.

The bets against the group have also escalated in recent days, with Acadian increasing its short position in the group.

It coincided with Mr Trump singling out the UK’s net zero policy, saying wind turbines were ruining the landscape and criticising high taxes on North Sea oil.

“I want to stop seeing them ruining that beautiful Scottish and English countryside with windmills and massive solar panels that go seven miles by seven miles, taking away farmland,” the US president told the UN on Tuesday.

Mr Trump described North Sea oil as “so highly taxed that no developer, no oil company can go there”.

“They’ve given up their powerful edge, a lot of the countries that we’re talking about, in oil and gas, such as essentially closing the great North Sea oil,” he said.

Victoria McCulloch, an analyst at RBC Capital, said a dearth of North Sea energy services companies for short-sellers to focus on was part of the reason for the high short interest in Ashtead.

She said the impact of Mr Trump’s tariffs and decreasing oil prices had forced Ashtead to cut revenue guidance earlier this year.

Net zero targets and windfall taxes have hammered offshore oil and gas activity in the North Sea, with projects stalling in recent years as successive governments prioritise net zero over oil and gas.

Last month, Jim Johnson, the chief executive of oil group Hunting, said he had been forced to close four of the company’s five UK centres including its main Scottish site because the Government had destroyed the North Sea offshore oil industry.

He said the closures were directly linked to government windfall taxes and over-zealous regulations imposed on the industry to reach net zero.

A spokesman from Ashtead Technology said: “As we look to move up to the Main Market of the London Stock Exchange, expected on Oct 6, we are wholly focused on executing against our proven growth strategy and assisting our customers with their critical subsea operations, with the support of our long-term shareholders.”

Citadel and Acadian were contacted for comment.

data.title: The secret reason why two ‘backward’ American states produce more cars each year than the whole UK
data.description:
Every day until Saturday, Douglas Carswell will be writing on the lessons that the UK can learn from the policy innovations driving growth in the American states.

Throughout the American Rust Belt – in the Midwest and Northeastern states – factories have closed, and jobs have vanished as industries have declined. But where did those jobs go? Not to China or Vietnam, but to the South.

From 2019 to 2023, the Rust Belt lost roughly 60,000 manufacturing jobs, while Texas alone gained almost 50,000. Jobs that disappeared from Pennsylvania, Indiana, and Ohio didn’t necessarily cross oceans – many of them relocated to states like Texas, Georgia, Tennessee, and South Carolina.

The real story of the American Rust Belt is not about cheap foreign competition taking American jobs. It is a story of states with increasingly rigid labour laws losing out to states – often in the South – that have made their labour markets more flexible.

Take Michigan. It has been the leading car manufacturing state since Henry Ford pioneered mass production. But production there has stalled.

It’s not really China that is to blame but Michigan’s ever more prescriptive labour laws, among other domestic factors. Over the past couple of decades, Michigan has adopted a raft of laws governing health and safety, wage payment schedules, and an array of entitlements. A couple of years ago, Michigan repealed so-called right-to-work laws, restoring mandatory union membership in some workplaces.

In contrast, states like Alabama, Texas, Tennessee and my own Mississippi have far more flexible labour markets. Employers can terminate the contracts of employees for almost any reason (or no reason) without notice, and employees can resign at any time. They obviously have to comply with minimum standards set by the federal government, but they don’t, for the most part, embellish upon them.

The result? Auto manufacturing in such states is booming. Michigan might still be number one, but since 2000 the number of auto manufacturing jobs in the state is down by around 40 per cent. In Alabama alone, the number of auto jobs has now surpassed 50,000, up from just a few thousand in 1997.

Despite all the talk of deindustrialisation in America, the US economy overall produces more manufactured goods today than ever before. Industrial output in the US today is approximately twice what it was when Ronald Reagan was in the White House, and three times greater than when Lyndon Johnson was president.

In Britain, however, labour laws over the past two decades have become increasingly restrictive. Instead of freeing employers to make the decisions for their businesses, the onus is on employers to justify letting staff go. Workers have enhanced protections. EU-era working time regulations remain in place and anti-discrimination laws are stringent. Employment tribunals often seem to side with employees. Resolving employment disputes costs employers billions each year.

This is surely one of the reasons industrial output has grown sclerotically over the past two decades. In 2023, UK manufacturing output was $279bn (8 per cent of GDP) compared to $237bn (12 per cent of GDP) in 2003.

Of course, labour markets are important not only for industrial jobs, but for every sort of employment.

Flexible employment laws generally mean more employment – which is partly why America has been so good at creating jobs in the first place. And those jobs seem to have been disproportionately created in states with more flexible labour laws.

Industrial jobs might have moved South in America, but so have many other types of employment. Since 2020, more than 150 financial firms, managing trillions of dollars, have relocated operations from the likes of New York and moved South, notably to Florida and North Carolina. JP Morgan Chase, one of America’s largest banks, now has a larger work force in Texas than in New York state.

Light touch labour laws also mean more jobs that can sustain a higher living standard. American workers are so much more productive that they are able to earn significantly more. By 2021, a British study estimated that a UK worker produced on average £46.92 per hour worked. Their American equivalent generated £58.88, approximately 20 per cent more value.

Since 2010, the productivity of American workers has improved by about 23 per cent. That essentially means for each hour worked, they are generating almost a quarter more. In the UK, by contrast, productivity has risen by less than 10 per cent. In large public sector organisations like the NHS, productivity has actually fallen in recent years.

Britain’s productivity performance over the past two decades has been dire, and all sorts of efforts have been made to address this, from Boris Johnson’s Levelling Up initiative to building high speed rail networks.

If Britain really wanted to boost productivity, a good place to start would be labour market reform. Having a flexible workforce allows firms to quickly adjust staffing to match demand, reducing idle labour and boosting output per worker.

When productivity increases, this also tends to push up wages. British wages were roughly on a par with those in the US 20 years ago. Factoring in lower US taxes, net wages in America may now be 40-50 per cent higher than in Britain.

And while the Left might expect workers to be fleeing states it considers to engage in exploitative labour practices, better living standards are a key reason why the opposite is occurring. States with more flexible markets have been among those enjoying the strongest population growth in recent years, as families leave tightly-regulated areas in the Northeast and Midwest for better opportunities in the South.

Britain desperately needs to learn the lessons of what works in America, and introduce, among other changes, a 90-day “at-will” dismissal period for new hires, allowing termination without cause, modelled on US employment law. Like Mississippi, and other southern states, it needs to make a concerted effort to remove many of the occupational licencing rules that, according to the Social Mobility Commission in 2024, mean some sort of licencing is required for 29 per cent of occupations, impacting almost a quarter of the workforce.

Post-Brexit Britain should stop comparing itself to the European Union, whose labour markets are even more inflexible, and deindustrialisation even more pronounced. Instead, British policy-makers should look to Texas, Tennessee and Mississippi labour reforms if it wants to have an industrial future.

Mississippi and her neighbour Alabama, two states with a combined population of about nine million that have regularly been dismissed as economically “backward”, are now estimated to produce more cars each year than the whole of the UK, with a population of 69 million. Perhaps instead of ignoring their lessons, British politicians should accept the evidence staring them in the face.

Douglas Carswell is the President & CEO of the Mississippi Center for Public Policy. He was previously the MP for Clacton

data.title: Boden ad campaign featuring dead salmon triggers angler backlash
data.description:
Clothing retailer Boden has triggered a backlash among British anglers after it launched a campaign featuring models posing as fishermen holding dead salmon.

Critics called the fashion shoot “irresponsible” at a time when salmon stocks in Britain’s rivers are at an all-time low.

According to the charity, Atlantic Salmon Trust, Britain has lost around 70pc of its Atlantic salmon stocks over the past few decades. The Game and Wildlife Conservation Trust warns that the species could become extinct within 30 years unless action is taken.

Joe Beale, a naturalist who owns a stretch of the River Orchy, posted on social media: “I don’t know one salmon fisherman who would kill a wild salmon today. They are nearly extinct especially on the west coast.”

Boden was also accused of using farmed salmon in the campaign, which fishermen say has worsened the decline of the UK’s salmon population because of sea lice, which breed in farms and latch on to wild species when they swim past.

The campaign, which was posted on Instagram, shows images and videos of models wearing Fair Isle-inspired knitwear and tartan kilts armed with fishing rods in the Welsh countryside.

You Fish Scotland, a fly fishing trip provider, commented on Boden’s Instagram post: “Serious questions need to be asked about the ethics here.”

Marina Gibson, of the Atlantic Salmon Trust, added: “I hardly know where to start. It just goes to show the majority of people are clueless about wild salmon.”

Boden was founded by old Etonian Johnnie Boden, a hunting enthusiast, in 1991 as a mail-order business. Known for its countryside chic and brightly-coloured knitwear, its styles have been worn by the Princess of Wales and Carrie Johnson.

Marcus Janssen, executive director of the country clothing brand Schoffel Country and a salmon angler, said the fish featured in the advertisement had “clearly come from a supermarket fish counter”.

Mr Janssen added that brands which purport to represent the countryside “have a huge responsibility to understand it”. He said: “This shoot is massively irresponsible when wild salmon stocks are so low.”

Geordie Doull, a professional fishing guide on the River Thurso, said “as a ghillie it makes me cross”.

He said that it was time salmon farms were brought on land rather than being in the sea with the wild fish.

Mr Doull added: “Atlantic salmon are in peril and the public don’t know that.”

Boden was contacted for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Revolut vows to invest £3bn in UK as it hunts banking licence
data.description:
Revolut has pledged to invest £3bn in Britain and create 1,000 jobs as it steps up efforts to gain a full banking licence.

Nik Storonsky, the financial technology start-up’s billionaire chief executive, said Revolut would invest the sum over the next five years. He was speaking on Tuesday as he opened the company’s new headquarters in Canary Wharf.

It is understood a significant part of the funding is tied up in efforts to fully capitalise its UK bank as it seeks to secure a full banking licence.

Revolut was granted a banking licence last year after a three-year wait. But the permit remains provisional, limiting the business from offering key lending products such as mortgages and from holding larger customer deposits.

The status of the London-based business’s banking licence has become highly political, amid claims that Rachel Reeves has sought to intervene in the process on the company’s behalf.

In July, Andrew Bailey, the Governor of the Bank of England, blocked an attempt by Ms Reeves to set up a meeting between Britain’s most valuable fintech company and City regulators in an effort to speed up approval.

The Chancellor tried to arrange the meeting as part of her attempts to persuade the $75bn (£55bn) UK-based company to list in London instead of New York.

Speaking on Tuesday, Mr Storonsky said Revolut was focused on establishing its UK bank: “We have 11 million customers in the UK, it is our number one market. So our number one priority is to roll them into the UK bank.”

Ms Reeves attended the opening of Revolut’s new headquarters at the YY building in the heart of London’s financial district. Ms Reeves said Revolut could “really help our country step up” its productivity.

She said: “For too long, Britain has been the laggard of productivity performance. Ever since the financial crisis, our productivity has lagged behind our competitors, and I’m committed to changing that, and I know that Revolut is as well.”

The Chancellor added that the investment pledge showed “the UK is well and truly open for business under this Government”.

The commitment to the UK comes on the back of a series of pledges by financial services giants to invest in Britain in the wake of Donald Trump’s state visit last week.

The Government says it has secured more than £110bn in investment from financial giants, including billions of pounds from investment giants Blackstone and BlackRock.

Revolut has been waiting for more than a year for a full banking licence. Mr Storonsky has previously criticised the UK’s banking regulator, accusing the City watchdog of being “extremely bureaucratic” and “extra cautious”.

He has also played down the prospect of Revolut launching an initial public offering on the London Stock Exchange, instead favouring a US listing. Mr Storonksy said a float in the city would not be “rational” for the fintech business.

The fintech giant has raised hundreds of millions of pounds and is in the process of securing further funding from venture capital investors and backers including Nvidia as part of a secondary share sale, which is expected to value Revolut at $75bn.

Revolut plans to invest $10bn and create more than 10,000 jobs around the world over the next five years, it said on Tuesday.

data.title: HMRC uses new powers to raid taxpayers’ bank accounts directly
data.description:
Tax inspectors have already started raiding the bank accounts of those who choose not to pay their bills, HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC) has admitted.

The taxman can claim money directly from the accounts of debtors who owe more than £1,000 under “direct recovery powers”, which were first introduced in 2015 but paused during the pandemic.

HMRC said on Monday it had reintroduced the strategy in a “test and learn” phase after being given the powers to do so by Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, in her Spring Statement.

Tax agents will first visit a taxpayer before any money is taken from their bank account or cash Isa. They must also ensure the debtor will be left with at least £5,000 and that the timetable for appeals has passed.

A report into the efficiency of direct recovery from 2019 estimated that it had generated an additional £178m in tax revenue.

However, it was used only 19 times in two years, from April 2016 to December 2018, and raised only £361,678 for the taxman.

Mike Warburton, The Telegraph’s tax expert, said: “I am very concerned about this development.

“Clearly taxpayers should pay the tax that is properly due, but I do not have the confidence that HMRC will always get it right.

“I recognise that there are safeguards in place, but this really is the thin edge of a wedge leading towards a very dangerous development.”

‘Draconian power’

Dawn Register, of accountancy firm BDO, said: “The relaunch of this draconian power underlines how important it is not to stick your head in the sand and ignore HMRC demands.

“HMRC needs to strike the right balance between supporting businesses and individuals in genuine financial difficulty, while being assertive with those who can afford to pay but choose not to.”

The Government is exploring a number of ways to improve how much data it has on taxpayers and how much they owe.

In August, HMRC admitted that it was using artificial intelligence to spy on taxpayers’ social media posts.

Savings accounts are also set for more scrutiny. From April 2027, banks will be required to ask both new and existing customers with savings accounts for their National Insurance numbers to make it easier for HMRC to bill taxpayers who breach their personal savings allowance.

The requirements, which will be introduced in legislation next year, will see more workers pay savings tax directly from their pay packets without submitting a self-assessment.

Ministers are also pushing ahead with plans to recover money directly from the accounts of benefit claimants.

The Department of Work and Pensions (DWP) will be granted new powers under the Public Authorities (Fraud, Error and Recovery) bill, which is due back in the House of Lords in October.

It is expected to give the DWP the power to inspect the bank accounts of benefits claimants for evidence of fraud and government error.

A DWP spokesman said it will not have direct access to benefit claimants’ bank accounts and that all new powers granted in the bill are “underpinned by a principle of fairness and proportionality, with numerous safeguards and independent oversight in place”.

An HMRC spokesman said: “Most people pay tax on time and in full – but it’s right that we seek to recover tax from the tiny minority who have the funds to pay, but refuse to.

“These powers are subject to robust safeguards and we’ll continue to support customers who need help with their payments.”

data.title: I have no sympathy for ‘victims’ of this mortgage scandal
data.description:
In the 1990s, following years of falling house prices, two banks came to market with a deal for homeowners.

It was a straightforward agreement with the terms clearly laid out: “We’ll lend you interest-free money against your home and if it increases in value we’ll take a cut of the appreciation – and you’ll never have to pay a penny until you sell.”

Almost three decades on, while the banks have held up their end of the bargain, homeowners are now crying foul.

After rejected ombudsman complaints and a group claim settled out of court, the disgruntled are once again taking legal action against the banks.

These so-called shared appreciation mortgages, offered for just a few years by Barclays and Bank of Scotland, are being decried by interested parties who point to eye-watering debts in the millions now owed to these “loan shark” financiers.

Homeowners could borrow up to 25pc of the value of their home then and would only have to repay when the time came to sell, paying the bank the principal sum and up to 75pc of the house price appreciation. If they borrowed less, they’d owe the bank less when the time came to sell – a 15pc loan would only grant the bank a 45pc share of the price difference, for example.

The general argument runs that the terms were unfair, repayments are punitive and the products were mis-sold. How could somebody who borrowed £137,500 possibly repay a £1.2m debt?

Well, given the home is worth almost £2m today, quite easily. The beneficiaries of the sale are still receiving around £800,000, a 45pc increase on the £550,000 value of the home when the loan was taken out.

I’m not certain I know of many other loans that offer you the chance to profit without ever being out of pocket.

Now, 45pc is quite a bit less than the average house price growth of 431pc since the beginning of 1996 – and here’s where the issue really lies. Nobody who took out these loans could’ve foreseen the extraordinary house price appreciation the UK has witnessed since. Even the brokers wouldn’t have factored this kind of return into their projections.

While their neighbours have enjoyed the phenomenal equity growth of their homes, to be withdrawn in later life or passed on through inheritance, affected homeowners have had to come to terms with the fact they took one marshmallow rather than waiting for two. Those in line to inherit will receive less than their peers.

This is nothing more than sour grapes.

Adult homeowners, who were required to seek legal advice before agreeing, bought a transparently advertised financial product and are now upset the bet went the way of the banks.

Had house prices fallen, the borrower enjoyed an incredibly generous deal. In the event of stagnating or falling values, they would have never owed a penny of interest to the bank – they would only have to pay the principal sum, which they could repay at whatever time they chose.

I have read the original brochure circulated by Barclays and it makes these terms perfectly clear. In fact, it even offers the one example in which the borrower would lose out – a moderate house price increase. A rise of 50pc against a 25pc loan would see the homeowner pay more than the total appreciation, as Barclays illustrated.

But this didn’t come to pass. House prices soared, homeowners became accidental millionaires – and some remodelled their kitchens in the late 1990s with borrowed money.

House price appreciation is not an inalienable right and buyer’s remorse is not a scandal. Sometimes, things don’t go your way.

data.title: The magic number of bank accounts you need to get richer
data.description:
Budgeting is one of the most effective ways to take control of your spending and grow your wealth in a sustainable manner. By separating your money into different categories, you can ensure everyday costs are covered while still setting aside funds for future goals, such as your next holiday or your retirement.

One simple way to do this is to open multiple bank accounts, each with a clear purpose. If you do well, this can help you stay disciplined, achieve your goals and grow your wealth more quickly.

Here, Telegraph Money explains the “magic number” of bank accounts to hold, how to use each one, and what to consider before opening them.

Account 1: For your income

This is the account where all your income lands. This can include your monthly salary, side-hustle earnings and rental income.

Once your funds have been deposited into this account, you can transfer them to your other bank accounts for various purposes.

Account 2: For your essential spending

This bank account is reserved for all the costs you can’t avoid, such as your mortgage or rent, food, utilities, insurance and transport.

Each month, transfer over a fixed amount to cover these costs. The golden rule is that once the money is in this account, it’s strictly for essentials, not discretionary spending.

Account 3: For your investing and saving

Use this account to help save for your future. The money here should go towards long-term goals such as investing in stocks and shares, index funds, property or contributing to your retirement savings.

As this account is for growing your wealth, any withdrawals made must be for these purposes only.

Account 4: For your fun spending

Finally, this account is for your guilt-free spending, whether that’s meals out, a shopping spree, a holiday or subscription payments.

Move across a fixed amount each week or month, and when the account runs dry, you can no longer spend. This ensures you enjoy discretionary spending without exceeding your budget.

Benefits of having multiple bank accounts

Having multiple bank accounts makes it easier to separate money for different goals and reduces the risk of spending from the “wrong” pot.

Will Fenton, of financial comparison site Sterling Savvy, said: “One of the biggest benefits of having multiple bank accounts is that it removes guesswork. You no longer have to mentally calculate what’s already committed to bills or savings and what’s actually available to spend. Your accounts already do that job for you.

“It also helps build habits that support long-term goals. If you’re putting money into a separate account for future plans, whether that’s saving, investing or paying down debt, it becomes a regular part of your routine.”

Additionally, opening multiple bank accounts allows you to pick and choose from a variety of perks and features.

Alice Haine, of investment platform Bestinvest, said: “Banks offer a number of incentives to attract new customers, from cashback and high interest rates on balances to lifestyle perks such as travel insurance or TV subscriptions.”

For example, a bank account that offers cashback on your household bills could suit your essential spending account, while an account with perks such as fee-free spending abroad, insurance coverage or cashback on shopping might be perfect for your fun spending account.

Many banks also offer cash bonuses to new customers when they open a bank account, which you may be able to benefit from.

Considerations when opening a bank account

Before opening a bank account, it’s important to review thoroughly the terms and conditions, fees, and eligibility criteria so that you understand what you’re signing up for.

Rajan Lakhani, of money app Plum, said: “You might not be entitled to or eligible for some benefits the account offers, depending on a variety of factors, like whether you’ve had the account before, if you need to deposit a certain amount of money, and so on. So, do take the time to check.”

Also, be aware that some providers limit the number of accounts you can hold with them, so you may need to open accounts with more than one provider. This can be beneficial anyway if you’re planning to store a lot of cash in your accounts, as the Financial Services Compensation Scheme only protects funds of up to £85,000 per person per financial institution.

Fraud is another risk to consider. Each additional account presents another vulnerability, so strong passwords and regular transaction monitoring are essential, according to Ms Haine.

She added: “While having several accounts won’t necessarily damage your credit score, opening several accounts in quick succession could have a temporary impact, especially if you apply for several overdrafts at once, as this may signal to lenders that you are under financial pressure. It’s best to space out applications.”

Tips for managing several bank accounts

Having more than one bank account comes with additional administration, and you’ll need to review each account regularly to ensure you’re receiving the expected benefits.

Mr Lakhani said: “If you’re worried about managing lots of accounts, many apps offer customers the option to link multiple bank accounts so you can see everything in one place. Just be sure to monitor that you’re still in receipt of your perks like cashback and check when any interest rates might change.”

Setting up direct debits or standing orders so that money is transferred from each account on payday will prevent you from forgetting and help you stick to your plan. Many banking apps also let you track balances, set spending alerts and round up transactions, which can help you stay on top of managing your accounts more easily.

data.title: Instagram boss: ‘We’re not eavesdropping on you’
data.description:
The head of Instagram has denied claims that the app eavesdrops on users’ conversations to sell adverts.

Adam Mosseri said he wanted to “set the record straight” on how the photo-sharing app targets adverts to its three billion users.

Tech giant Meta’s social media apps Instagram and Facebook have long been dogged by the myth that they listen to people’s phone microphones to sell adverts, with social media users claiming that products or companies they have mentioned in conversation crop up later as digital adverts. Mr Mosseri said this would be a “gross violation of privacy”.

Mr Mosseri said: “We do not listen to you. We do not use the phone’s microphone to eavesdrop on you.”

Instagram’s chief said there were several reasons ads might appear on the app around the same time they cropped up in conversation.

This could be because the user tapped an advert or searched online for the product, since Meta and other advertisers share data about their audiences across their digital networks.

Adverts can also appear if a user’s friends show an interest in certain products. Mr Mosseri said: “It could be that you were talking to someone about a product, and they had actually looked for or searched for that product. Or that, in general, people with similar interests were doing the exact same thing.”

He also said that sometimes people might see an advert on Instagram and not realise it, before bringing up that product in conversation. “Sometimes you internalise some of that,” he said.

Users might also see adverts about products they have spoken about by random chance – with hundreds of millions of users being served adverts every day. “Random chance, coincidence, it happens,” he said.

Meta has announced plans to use conversations users hold with its AI chatbots to influence what adverts they are shown. The tech giant has flooded its apps with new AI features and chatbots such as its Meta AI tool.

However, Meta said it would not use these conversations to target in Europe or the UK.

Meta does, however, now provide an alternative to targeted adverts on Facebook – launching an option to pay a £3.99 subscription to avoid personalised ads on its social media apps.

data.title: Don’t tax growth out of existence, Barclays boss tells Reeves
data.description:
The boss of Barclays has urged Rachel Reeves not to tax economic growth “out of existence” amid fears the Chancellor will raise levies in November’s Budget.

In particular, CS Venkatakrishnan warned the Chancellor against “milking the financial sector” by introducing a new tax on banks.

In an interview with CNBC, he said: “It stifles competition, stifles growth. You need to encourage it to grow, not tax it out of existence.”

The comments come after the Institute for Public Policy Research (IPPR) think tank, which is seen as being close to the Government, called for a windfall levy on the banking sector.

Speculation of higher taxes on landlords, investors and higher earners is also rife, with Ms Reeves facing a gap in the public finances widely thought to be worth tens of billions of pounds.

One estimate, which Ms Reeves has rejected, put the figure as high as £50bn.

In a research paper, the IPPR argued the move would help recover losses for the taxpayer that have resulted from the Bank of England’s quantitative easing programme after the financial crisis.

This essentially saw the Bank create money and use it to buy bonds from the banking sector, giving them reserves on which they then accrued interest.

Now, Threadneedle Street is selling off those bonds at a loss and paying out higher interest rates to the banks.

The IPPR says these losses are worth £22bn a year and urged a tax on deposits similar to that launched by Margaret Thatcher in the 1980s.

News of the think tank’s proposals earlier this year sent shares in listed British banks, including Lloyds, Barclays, HSBC and Natwest tumbling, knocking billions of pounds off their combined worth.

Mr Venkatakrishnan has also previously criticised the idea of a windfall tax for having “facile and fallacious logic”.

He said the tax would result in less hiring and “less credit into the UK economy” from banks, which would be forced to rein in lending to British businesses.

Charlie Nunn, chief executive of Britain’s largest lender Lloyds, also previously urged Ms Reeves against a tax raid, claiming it would damage the country’s growth ambitions.

On Friday, a Treasury spokesman said: “We are a pro-business government, and the Chancellor has been clear that the financial services sector is at the heart of our plans to grow the economy. 

“This is why we have taken action to boost investment, such as the Leeds Reforms, which is cutting red tape to boost the City’s competitiveness and has put the UK on course to be the number one destination for financial services firms by 2035.”

data.title: Tesco boss tells Reeves: ‘Enough is enough’ on tax rises
data.description:
The boss of Tesco has told Rachel Reeves that “enough is enough” on tax rises amid growing fears she will launch a fresh raid on businesses to plug a hole in Britain’s finances.

Ken Murphy, the retailer’s chief executive, urged the Chancellor not to “make it harder” for supermarkets to keep prices down when she delivers her Budget next month.

Tesco said it had been dealt a £235m blow from Ms Reeves’s decision to raise National Insurance contributions paid by employers this year, while a new packaging levy will cost it £90m.

Mr Murphy said: “What we’d love to see from the next Budget is a Budget that’s pro-growth and pro-jobs, which as a result will help customers with the cost of living.

“We know that people are worried about what lies ahead and we’re seeing that in the consumer sentiment. We operate in a very, very competitive and a very tough environment.

“Our one ask is, don’t make it harder for the industry to deliver great value for customers.

“In the last Budget, the industry and the sector incurred substantial additional operating costs. We’re doing our best to deal with them – but enough is enough.”

Mr Murphy’s comments come days after the Chancellor warned that her second Budget would not be easy for families and businesses as she left the door open to another tax raid on corporate Britain.

While Ms Reeves acknowledged it was important that Britain’s tax regime was “internationally competitive”, she added: “I think businesses recognise, like everybody does, that paying taxes is important to support the economy.”

Mr Murphy said the Chancellor needed to be careful on measures such as the upcoming business rates reform, which threatened to raise costs further for large supermarkets.

He said Tesco would wait to see the details until it makes a decision on any store closures.

Mr Murphy was speaking as Tesco raised its forecasts for the year, saying it now expected profits of between £2.9bn and £3.1bn for the year to February. It had previously been expecting profits of between £2.7bn and £3bn.

This followed a period where Tesco won over more customers, having stepped up price cuts in response to growing competition from Asda. The rival supermarket launched a price war earlier this year in an attempt to revive its fortunes.

Tesco said it has cut the prices of 6,500 products with an average reduction of around 9pc. It said it was matching Aldi on more than 600 lines.

The supermarket said this had helped it to win more customers, with the company now holding 28.4pc of the grocery market. Sales were up 4.3pc in the first six months of the year. Profits slipped 9.6pc to £950m in the period after it had previously forecasted a decline in earnings.

data.title: Runaway oil tycoon re-emerges to deny knowledge of £1.5bn debt pile
data.description:
The runaway boss of a failed oil refinery has re-emerged to claim he does not know how much the company owes to creditors.

Winston Soosaipillai, who ran Lindsey oil refinery owner Prax, said he cannot provide details of the company’s £1.5bn debt pile, nor does he know how much money the company still has after its collapse earlier this year.

In his first public commentary since the downfall of his energy empire, Mr Soosaipillai told Prax’s administrators: “I am without the direct knowledge necessary for concurring with the outstanding balances owed to creditors.”

That is despite his role as chairman and chief executive of Prax before its collapse. He was also the company’s owner alongside his wife, Arani Soosaipillai.

Instead, he told the High Court that he was solely reliant on the financial information provided by Ben Lahnstein, the company’s chief financial officer.

The admission was made in a so-called statement of truth signed by Mr Soosaipillai earlier this month. He also said he was unable to confirm “the values of the company’s assets”.

It is the first time Mr Soosaipillai has made any comments regarding Prax’s failure, which has put hundreds of jobs at risk and raised concerns over Britain’s fuel supplies.

Authorities had previously been unable to contact Mr Soosaipillai in the wake of his company’s demise, with calls and emails going unanswered. His whereabouts have largely been unknown.

Administrators were drafted to oversee Prax’s unexpected failure in June. At the time Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, launched an urgent investigation and said he was concerned about “the conduct of directors”.

The administrators of Prax have since issued a lawsuit against Mr Soosaipillai – in which they have accused him of deceit and misrepresentation. Court documents have also claimed a breach of contract and raised concerns about a loan facility.

A report into the company’s demise on Companies House recently blamed “material irregularities” that were discovered in a £783m loan agreement with HSBC.

While Mr Soosaipillai has sought to keep a low profile since putting hundreds of jobs at risk, The Telegraph previously revealed that the oil tycoon still owned a £5m property in Surrey.

A long list of creditors is owed vast sums following Prax’s collapse, including £250m to HMRC. High-profile creditors include Shell – which is owed £9.2m – as well as Uber and Rentokil.

The complex nature of Mr Soosaipillai’s business empire means hundreds of millions of pounds are also owed to some of his other energy companies, which include petrol stations and other oil and gas operations.

Administrators are now trying to unpick the company’s remaining assets to see how much can be distributed to the creditors, if anything. In correspondence to the administrators, Mr Lahnstein has suggested that this will amount to at least £11.8m.

Union chiefs recently warned that the site is at risk of a “catastrophe” following an exodus of workers.

In a letter to Mr Miliband, Unite the Union said the site – which is currently under the control of the Government’s insolvency arm – is in an “unsafe and dangerous state”.

The Lindsey refinery was previously part of the wider Prax Group, which includes some 200 petrol stations plus oil and gas operations west of the Shetland Islands.

It supplied petrol, diesel, fuel oil, kerosene, aviation fuel and bitumen, and Mr Soosaipillai purchased the facility from French oil giant Total in 2021.

The administrators declined to comment.

data.title: Jaguar Land Rover signals end to month-long factory shutdown
data.description:
Jaguar Land Rover (JLR) will restart parts of its manufacturing lines in the coming days after a devastating cyber attack.

The carmaker has told suppliers and staff that “some sections” of its production operations would soon return to normal following the hack on 31 August, which brought its factories to a standstill.

A JLR spokesman said: “We are taking further steps towards our recovery and the return to manufacture of our world-class vehicles.

“Today we are informing colleagues, retailers and suppliers that some sections of our manufacturing operations will resume in the coming days.”

The carmaker has been racing to bring its IT systems and manufacturing technology back online after it was forced to halt production at a cost of hundreds of millions of pounds.

The cyber attack has also had a knock-on effect on hundreds of businesses that supply parts to the car giant.

Thousands of workers in JLR’s supply chain have been told to stay at home for weeks. Some have been laid off or told to sign on for Universal Credit. Problems with JLR’s payments systems have also meant invoices have been left unpaid.

Last week, JLR made its first steps towards a return to normal operations, reopening a key logistics hub used to get parts to car dealers and repair workshops. The Telegraph reported on Friday evening that suppliers had been told to stand ready for deliveries to resume from Monday.

On Saturday, Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, confirmed the Government would step in to guarantee a £1.5bn emergency loan to JLR to accelerate payments to its supply chain. The loan is provided by a commercial bank but underwritten by the taxpayer.

Mr Kyle said: “This loan guarantee will help support the supply chain and protect skilled jobs in the West Midlands, Merseyside and throughout the UK.”

On Monday, India’s Economic Times newspaper reported that JLR was close to raising up to £2bn from global banks, in addition to the taxpayer-backed loan.

The newspaper reported that banks including Citi and Standard Chartered were in talks over the debt facility as the carmaker seeks to secure additional liquidity to weather the shutdown. The banks and a spokesman for JLR declined to comment.

The cyber attack on JLR is the latest in a hacking spree against British businesses this year. The attacks have been claimed by a gang calling itself Scattered Lapsus$ Hunters, believed to be made up of British and American teenagers. Members of the group were also blamed for a cyber attack on Marks & Spencer in April.

A JLR spokesman said: “We know there is much more to do but the foundational work of our recovery is firmly underway and we will continue to provide updates as we progress.”
