data.title: France leads revolt against Chinese fast fashion giant
data.description:
As designers celebrated craftsmanship and couture on the catwalks at Paris Fashion Week, Shein, the Chinese fast fashion giant, dropped a sartorial bombshell.

The e-commerce colossus, whose business model is built on churning out thousands of new garments every day at dirt-cheap prices, has revealed that Paris will house its first physical store.

The store, to open at BHV Marais department store next month, will be followed by concessions in five Galeries Lafayette department stores in Dijon, Reims, Grenoble, Angers and Limoges.

The timing and location could hardly be more provocative. France is Europe’s fashion capital and also the country leading a political revolt against the business model Shein represents.

For critics, the announcement felt like a deliberate intrusion: fast fashion planting its flag in the heartland of haute couture.

‘Hundreds of stores have been closed’

“They’re taking over everything,” said Yann Rivoallan, president of the French Federation of Women’s Ready-to-Wear, on the radio channel France Inter.

Pointing out that the BHV Marais store was right in front of Paris’s town hall, he added: “They’re taking up all the space, both in the media and on the internet, and now they also want to take up physical space in the most beautiful locations.”

Mr Rivoallan also accused Shein of decimating the Gallic retail landscape.

He said: “For more than three years now, Shein has destroyed 15,000 jobs and dozens, even hundreds, of stores have been closed throughout France.

“To dare to say now that they are going to revitalise is the worst possible thing in terms of perversion and communication.”

Shein made the announcement as politicians in Paris debated a “bonus-malus” system that would penalise disposable fashion and reward sustainable production. The draft bill also wants to ban Shein and other ultra-fast fashion groups from advertising.

In July, a French court fined the company €40m (£35m) for misleading customers on discounts and claims the company made about its commitment to the environment.

The Chinese-founded, Singapore-based group became one of the biggest fashion retailers in the world during the pandemic, but has faced virulent criticism over the environmental impact of its business model and allegations of forced labour in its supply chain. Shein has denied the allegations.

Antoine Vermorel-Marques, the Republican MP promoting the legislation, accused Shein of cynically trying to buy legitimacy.

He said: “I think they’re trying to buy themselves a good reputation. With resources and money, you can buy beautiful, symbolic locations.

“Their challenge is not to sell at BHV or Galeries Lafayette – that’s just communication. Their challenge is to continue flooding us with their online platform to undermine our city centre shops.”

Donald Tang, Shein’s executive chairman, has claimed the strategy aims to pay tribute to France’s global fashion status.

He told French newspaper Le Figaro: “By choosing France as a testing ground for physical retail, we are sending a strong message.

“Our choice is a tribute to the country and to Paris, the fashion capital of the world and the birthplace of the modern department store.”

He also denied Shein bore responsibility for the collapse of traditional retail.

“If sales at traditional clothing stores have declined, it’s not because of Shein. The difficulties they face began long before we arrived,” Mr Tang said.

“Our ambition is not to replace them, but to work alongside them by providing solutions, generating footfall in stores and helping to maintain the vitality of shopping centres.” He promised 200 new jobs would follow.

‘We will not stop fast fashion’

Behind the deal is Frédéric Merlin, head of Société des Grands Magasins, which owns BHV and several Galeries Lafayette franchises.

“Shein has 25 million French customers! We asked ourselves how we could transform this extraordinary online business machine into a benefit for physical retail,” Mr Merlin told Le Parisien.

“The wheels are in motion: we will not stop fast fashion. The challenge is to ensure that it also serves the interests of traditional retailers. With physical stores, they will have the same weapons as their detractors,” he said, hailing the potential to rejuvenate provincial department stores and draw younger crowds.

But Galeries Lafayette’s parent company, which no longer owns BHV but still operates its flagship Paris site, expressed “profound disagreement” with the deal and vowed to block Shein from its directly owned stores.

The row is the latest in a series of political skirmishes around Shein and its rival Temu.

In the US, Donald Trump’s administration has scrapped its “de minimis” exemption for parcels under $800, and the European Union plans to remove its equivalent duty waiver on e-commerce parcels worth less than €150.

Britain – where Shein sales hit £2.05bn in 2024, a 32.3 per cent increase from the previous year – is also reviewing its policy on low-value imports after retailers said it was giving online players like Shein and Temu an unfair advantage.

Shein has already staged UK pop-ups and opened offices in London as it hopes to be listed on the London Stock Exchange. Proponents say the move would be a major boost to the UK capital, which has seen a number of high-profile companies leave the city for the US.

The British Fashion Council said a stock exchange listing for Shein would be “of significant concern to UK fashion designers and retailers”.

data.title: Just 19 new-build homes sold in Sadiq Khan’s London in May
data.description:
Just 19 newly built private homes were sold across the whole of London in May according to the latest data from the Land Registry.

In the latest sign of the capital’s housing crisis under Sir Sadiq Khan, the monthly sales figures were the lowest on record.

They were down from 23 sales in April and reflected a 95pc drop compared to May in 2024 when 352 flats and houses were sold.

The decline in sales has huge implications for Sir Sadiq’s plans to improve London’s housing market, while also raising doubts over the Mayor of London’s pledge to build 88,000 homes per year.

The new figures came after separate findings revealed on Thursday that the average London property is unaffordable for almost all households, according to the Office for National Statistics (ONS).

The ONS said: “In London, the average home was not affordable for any household income decile.”

The Home Builders Federation (HBF), which represents housebuilders in England and Wales, had earlier said the Mayor’s “unrealistic” affordable housing targets were “strangling” attempts to build new homes.

In a report on Monday, Neil Jefferson, HBF’s chief, said Sir Sadiq’s development strategy for the capital has “effectively made London a no-go zone for housing investment”.

The HBF said that the capital’s persistent shortage of new homes has made them “steadily more unaffordable over several decades”, pushing “more households into temporary accommodation and locking younger generations out of home ownership”.

The typical deposit for a home in London is equal to 681pc of the average annual income after bills, according to the HBF – demonstrating the lack of affordability.

It also estimated that first-time buyers in London would need to save 50pc of their discretionary income for more than 13 and a half years for a deposit.

At the same time, planning approvals in the capital have fallen by 2pc in the 12 months to June – the lowest total since records began in 2006.

All of which casts serious doubt over the Mayor’s pledge to build up to 19,000 affordable homes by March 2026.

Figures from the Greater London Authority revealed last month that construction started on just 347 affordable homes between April and June this year.

The Mayor of London’s office has blamed the “disastrous inheritance” from the previous Conservative government for making it more difficult to build homes.

A spokesman for the Mayor of London said: “The Mayor is doing everything in his power to deliver more homes of all tenures, taking hard decisions including actively exploring development on the green belt.

“While the impact of this legacy is being felt acutely in London due to rising construction costs, wider economic pressures and delays from the Building Safety Regulator, the Mayor is determined to work hand-in-hand with the Government to support their ambition to get Britain building again.”

data.title: How to build (and sell) a house in your garden
data.description:
If you’re lucky enough to have a generous garden with a bit of land going spare, you may have given some thought to coming up with a way to monetise the plot.

As part of this, you may have contemplated using that area to build a new home – or maybe even new homes – if space allows.

Do this successfully and you have the potential to unlock significant value by eventually selling the property, or properties, on to someone else.

However, you will need to be prepared to navigate a lot of hurdles, as building in your own garden is rarely straightforward.

While there is one set of rules to get your head around if you are planning on selling your land to a developer, here we take a look at the ins and outs if you want to do things yourself.

We will look at:

  • What planning permission do you need to build homes in your garden?
  • How much space do you need?
  • What else do you need to consider?
  • How do you finance the build?
  • Selling to a developer vs self-build – which can be more profitable?
  • Ways to minimise your tax bill

What planning permission do you need to build homes in your garden?

One key thing you need to know at the outset is that building a property on your land is not as easy as putting up an extension or garden office.

In short, this is because a self-contained dwelling is not classed as a permitted development.

Simon Rix, a planning specialist for the Homebuilding & Renovating Show, said: “To build a new house in your garden, you almost always require full planning permission. This is a major development that constitutes a material change of use of the land, and it is not covered by permitted development rights.”

The planning application submission fee for a new single dwelling on a small site is £588.

But be warned: this fee is a tiny fraction of the total cost. Mr Rix added: “The majority of the expense comes from professional fees for essential consultants, such as a planning consultant to manage the application, a designer or an architect to design the property and potentially also tree and/or ecologist consultants. These fees can easily reach at least £5,000.”

On top of planning costs, build costs can vary widely.

Shepherd Ncube, the founder of Springbok Properties, said: “A realistic figure is between £1,750 and £3,000 per square metre in the current climate, depending on complexity and finish.”

How much space do you need?

While an extra property could end up being a valuable asset that you eventually sell to free up some cash, you need to be sure it will fully make up for the loss of outdoor space.

Jeremy Leaf, a former RICS residential chairman, said: “If you use up most of the existing garden on the build, will that negatively impact the value not just of the new dwelling but also your existing home?”

According to some experts, if your garden is substantially bigger than the property’s footprint, reducing the garden size by an acre rarely affects the main property’s value. A lot will depend on just how ‘generous’ your plot is.

Equally, before you get ahead of yourself, remember it’s not simply a question of square metres: it’s about the quality and nature of the space.

Mr Rix said: “The key planning considerations are privacy and amenity space. A new dwelling must not cause unacceptable overlooking of neighbouring properties – or the existing house.”

It must also have sufficient private amenity space (a garden) for its future occupants.

Mr Rix added: “While there’s no set minimum size, the plot must be large enough to accommodate the new building without it feeling cramped or out of character with the surrounding area.

Most local authorities want at least as much garden as a house, but in some places, more garden will be needed than that – it will often depend on the prevailing pattern of development in the immediate area.”

What else do you need to consider?

Access is one of the most critical factors you need to think about

Mr Rix said: “The new dwelling will need a safe and functional access to a public highway for both vehicles and pedestrians. The local authority will be particularly concerned with visibility splays and highway safety.”

Not owning your drive can be problematic and rights of way can also be an issue if there’s potential for disputes.

Does the land need to be flat?

Mr Rix said: “While a flat site is easier and cheaper to build on, a sloped site is not a deal-breaker. It will simply require a more creative design and more expensive foundation work.”

Other key considerations include:

  • Drainage and utilities: ensuring there is a way to connect the new property to water, electricity and the mains sewer, if possible. If not, an on-site, small foul water treatment plant can be acceptable.
  • Impact on trees: The presence of protected trees or those with Tree Preservation Orders (TPOs) on or near the site could prevent development – or require a specific, well-thought-out design.
  • Neighbour amenity: you must show that the new home will not negatively impact neighbours in terms of loss of light, privacy or outlook.

Mr Ncube said: “In general, local planning authorities will judge a project on design and policy fit, looking closely at aspects such as privacy, daylight, parking, servicing, access, amenity space, drainage, fire safety and the overall impact on neighbours.

“Other things like TPOs, flood and drainage assessments, waste servicing guidance requirements, sustainable urban drainage systems and conservation areas must all be considered – and may constrain plans.”

How do you finance the build?

When it comes to funding the project, lenders will typically require the land title to be split.

Cristian Hinzpeter, of mortgage broker SPF Private Clients, said: “That way, they can take a charge against the land – rather than the house.”

This can also make a future sale easier.

Mr Hinzpeter added: “If there’s already a mortgage in place, splitting the title may trigger partial repayment – sometimes with penalty charges – so it’s worth checking whether a second charge might be more cost-effective and whether your current lender permits this.”

In terms of mortgage options, some lenders offer a hybrid arrangement.

Mr Hinzpeter said: “This might be a part-residential mortgage, to cover the existing loan, topped up with a self-build facility.”

Specialist advice from a mortgage broker is essential to help you navigate your financing options properly.

Selling to a developer vs self-build – which can be more profitable?

If you’re getting serious about the idea of monetising your garden with a new dwelling or two, you need to decide whether to sell to a developer or go it alone.

Mr Hinzpeter said: “Developers typically need larger profit margins, whereas self-build can be more cost-effective. With the right finance structure in place – and particularly with builder or contractor agreements – self-build can be much less stressful.”

This is a view shared by Mr Rix. He said: “Self-building is potentially far more profitable, as you capture the full value uplift from gaining planning permission and managing the construction.”

That said, you also take on all of the risk, financial outlay and time commitment.

Mr Rix added: “Selling to a developer is a much lower-risk, lower-reward approach. The developer will pay you an agreed price – often subject to planning permission being granted – and will take on all the subsequent risk and costs of the build.”

In addition to selling to a developer and deciding to self-build, there is a further option to think about: selling to individual self-builders or custom builders. Custom builds offer a kind of middle ground between self-build and traditional new-build, with much of the heavy lifting done by a developer. For some homeowners, this third approach can make the most sense.

Once again, doing your homework and working out what makes the most sense for you is key.

How to minimise your tax bill

If you do end up going ahead, you may be keen to know whether there’s the possibility of reducing capital gains tax (CGT) when you come to sell.

Shaun Moore, of Quilter, said: “The treatment of building and selling a property in your garden will depend on how the land is used and your intentions for the new home.”

If your plan is to sell the new property for profit, HMRC may view the activity as trading. Mr Moore said: “In that case, profits could be taxed as income, rather than capital gains, which would likely be much more punitive.”

If, on the other hand, you hold the property as a long-term investment, CGT treatment is more likely.

Mr Ncube said: “Tax is an area where homeowners need to tread carefully. If you sell the new property, CGT will usually apply. If, however, the home has genuinely been your main residence, private residence relief can be claimed.”

But just how long do you need to live there for this to happen?

Mr Ncube said: “There is no statutory minimum occupation period in this case, but what really matters is the quality of occupation. The key is genuine residence – for example, evidence of council tax and utilities, and being registered to the electoral roll. It’s important to note that token attempts to live in the new property purely to reduce tax liability are likely to be challenged by HMRC.”

While there’s no official fixed minimum time you need to live in the property for, you could end up in hot water if you only move in briefly – with the clear intention of avoiding tax.

As mentioned above, in some cases where the project looks like a trade – buying, building and selling with a profit motive – HMRC may assess income tax instead.

Mr Moore added: “Keeping detailed records and seeking professional advice as early as possible in the process will be essential to ensure the correct tax treatment is applied.”

You must think carefully

If you’re still keen to proceed, you need to ensure you enter into any project on this scale with your eyes wide open.

As part of this, it’s essential to take independent, professional, legal, financial and tax advice.

Mr Ncube warned: “While developing your garden can be an excellent way to unlock both lifestyle and financial benefits, success will often depend on thorough preparation, a clear understanding of planning, design and access constraints, as well as a realistic assessment of whether to self-build or whether to simply secure permission and sell on.”

data.title: Wasted wind power costs Britain £1bn
data.description:
Britain has spent more than £1bn on wasted wind power this year, raising questions over Ed Miliband’s plan to build thousands of new turbines across the UK.

The spiralling cost of switching off wind farms has come to light in new figures from tracker website Wasted Wind. It shows that the UK had spent £734m by this point in 2024.

This year’s bill has already topped £1bn because of growing strain on the electricity grid, which forces wind farms in remote areas to switch off when the network is too congested to transport their power elsewhere. This is known in the industry as curtailment.

At the same time, the imbalance requires energy officials to spend vast sums on replacement power, often from gas-fired plants.

The latest figures highlight the problems posed by Mr Miliband’s net zero push, as the Energy Secretary has vowed to accelerate the roll-out of renewables to help deliver green electricity across the UK by 2030.

So-called curtailment costs are ultimately passed on to customers via increases in their bills.

Regulator Ofgem recently warned that curtailment added around £15 to household bills as part of its latest price cap, which came into force on Wednesday.

A typical household will now pay up to £1,755 on gas and electricity a year, up from £1,720.

The increase has piled further pressure on Mr Miliband, who has vowed to cut household energy bills by £300 by the end of the decade.

Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary, has branded the pledge a fantasy. She said: “By 2030, we will be paying £8bn to wind farms, not to generate any energy but simply to turn off when it’s too windy.

“The British people want us to prioritise cheap energy over wind developers’ profit and that’s what the Conservatives will do.”

Sharon Graham, general secretary of the Unite union, has branded Mr Miliband’s net zero plan a disaster and called on Sir Keir Starmer, the Prime Minister, to sack him.

Price pain

Her comments were made just hours after separate figures showed that Britain’s industry is paying the highest electricity prices in the developed world.

The UK is also the second-most expensive country for household electricity, with bills more than double those in the US.

Mr Miliband has repeatedly denied that net zero is to blame for higher bills and branded critics such as Nigel Farage, the leader of Reform UK, as “ideological extremists”.

Reform has vowed to scrap net zero if it wins the next election, while suggesting that it would look to restart fracking.

Speaking at the Labour Party conference, Mr Miliband said: “Fracking will not take a penny off bills, it will not create long-term sustainable jobs, it will trash our climate commitments, and it is dangerous and deeply harmful to our natural environment.

“The good news is communities have fought back and won that fight before and can do so again.”

He added: “We sent those frackers packing, I say let’s ban fracking and let’s vow to send this bunch of frackers packing too.”

Mr Miliband also insisted that clean energy offered the best chance to reduce Britain’s reliance on volatile gas prices.

Richard Tice, the deputy leader of Reform, said: “Labour has spent over £1bn paying to turn off wind turbines while relying on gas plants instead.

“This proves we remain heavily dependent on gas and that the Government’s fixation on green energy is costing taxpayers billions, driving up energy prices and pushing the country deeper into financial crisis.

“It’s time Ed Miliband dropped this reckless net zero agenda and backed a serious energy strategy: North Sea drilling, modular nuclear reactors and shale gas.

“To be a rich nation you need cheap energy. Net zero gives us the opposite.”

data.title: London’s ‘dark heart of capitalism’ goes woke
data.description:
With a history dating back centuries, the City of London Corporation has long been viewed as a bastion of conservatism and tradition.

However, in an attempt to shrug off its stale, male and pale reputation, the Square Mile has recently embraced a range of liberal ideas and put itself at the forefront of Britain’s culture wars.

Once described by those on the Left as the capital’s “rotten borough”, corrupted by corporate capitalism and greed, the City is now on a mission to reinvent itself.

Its most recent attempt includes a proposal to become the “most faith-friendly business district in the world”, raising money to do so from the companies within it.

By charging businesses a “subscription-based” funding model, the City has promised a “Deliveroo-style mobile chaplaincy service” that office workers can use to book a priest, rabbi or imam.

The service would be part of a new faith centre in the City, drawing inspiration from rivals such as Canary Wharf, which funds its faith services with a levy of 1p per square metre on commercial rents.

Such actions shed light on how the corporation, which is the governing body for the financial district, is increasingly trying to move away from its image of an old boys’ club to adopt a more progressive approach.

However, in some quarters the faith drive has been met with bemusement.

Peter Rees, a professor at UCL’s Bartlett School of Planning and once the City’s chief planning officer for nearly 30 years, says: “It comes as a surprise to hear that the City Corporation will soon be peddling religion.

“I knew the Square Mile was in competition with Wall Street but hadn’t realised it was now taking on Vatican City as well.”

It is not the first time the City’s actions have perplexed onlookers.

Campaigners have recently targeted the City of London over its decision to allow transgender women to use the Kenwood Ladies’ Pond on Hampstead Heath.

That is despite a Supreme Court ruling in April that determined trans women are not legally women.

Sex Matters, a charity campaigning for sex-based rights, filed a High Court challenge against the Corporation in August, contesting its refusal to reconsider its policy.

The legal pressure has since prompted officials to launch a public consultation.

Previously, the City also attracted scrutiny amid the Black Lives Matter movement in 2021, which prompted the corporation to set up an anti-racism task force.

The task force subsequently called for the removal of statues depicting prominent City figures who had links to slavery, ultimately gaining support from the corporation to do so.

“Oh typical woke Lefties of the er, City of London Corporation,” said David Henig, of the think tank European Centre for International Political Economy, at the time.

However, it was only after then-housing secretary Robert Jenrick intervened that the City backpedalled, instead putting up signs next to the relevant statues to explain their historical wrongdoing.

All of which could not be further from the City’s reputation as the “dark heart of predatory capitalism”, as once claimed by John McDonnell, the former Labour shadow chancellor.

Martha Grekos, an alderwoman at the corporation, says the City is “always trying to find a purpose for its survival”, especially after Left-wing calls for its abolition.

This has accelerated efforts to bring people together, she says, rather than focusing purely on growth and economic dynamism.

Its latest “Faith in the City” project, based on a report commissioned by the corporation, is a clear attempt to strengthen that position, but there are questions over how much impact it would have.

After all, the research itself shows the share of City workers without a religion is the fastest-growing group within the district, having rocketed from 19pc to 43pc in the two decades to 2024.

However, Mark Gettleson, who is leading the project, argued in a City meeting in September that the Square Mile was an outlier among London’s local authorities for lacking an official interfaith forum.

In the report, City workers said they were forced to pray in stairwells or unused meeting rooms owing to a lack of available space, and said they felt uncomfortable mentioning fasting or prayer at work.

Gettleson added that even the churches, as established faith groups in the district, had “difficulty… reaching City workers at scale”.

But others are more cynical of the City’s motives.

Graeme Harrower, a former City councillor and a vocal critic, says: “Through this latest initiative, the corporation is promoting the practice of faith to make itself look good.”

A spokesman for the City of London Corporation said the institution was “embedding equality, equity, diversity, and inclusion across everything we do, with a dedicated EEDI function led by a specialist equalities director”.

They added: “Whether you see the City of London Corporation as a global voice for financial and professional services ... as a cultural leader and one of the country’s largest investors in the arts, or as custodian of the Square Mile and 11,000 acres of award-winning open spaces, we deliver for our communities and for the nation.

“Our strength lies in a unique blend of tradition and innovation – a heritage that attracts visitors and investors from across the world, and a convening power that is second to none.”

However, when even the world’s oldest municipal authority is caught up in modern-day culture wars, it seems no organisation will be able to avoid them.

data.title: ‘I bought several timeshares to shield my cash from inheritance tax – have I made a mistake?’
data.description:
Email your tax questions to Mike at: taxhacks@telegraph.co.uk.*

Dear Mike,

We have various timeshares and have recently bought some more – partly so our children can enjoy the benefits after we have gone, and to help reduce our savings capital.

We had the understanding that our timeshare contract grants the right to occupy for a certain number of years, but not the right of ownership, and that the timeshares would not be classified as an asset for inheritance tax purposes. However, I’ve recently read that they may be included in estate assets for inheritance tax purposes. Can you give a view on this?

Regards,

– John

Dear John,

When carrying out an inheritance tax planning exercise, we typically consider the physical assets we own – such as cash, shares and property. The law, however, goes much further than that.

As explained in the HMRC manuals at IHTM04030, it covers almost all types of assets, including any rights and interests which are legally enforceable. Your estate at death can even include assets you do not own personally, such as interests in a trust. From 2027, this will also include investments held by trustees in a defined contribution pension scheme.

Timeshares come in two forms. In one type, buyers become partial owners of the property through a fractional timeshare scheme. The alternative arrangement is that you purchase a legal right to stay on the property for a set period each year without owning a part of it. As you describe it, John, I think you have the latter arrangement. Nevertheless, in both cases, you have an asset that will form part of your estate at death.

When someone dies, their executors must submit details to HMRC, which include a list of all assets. Most of these will have a known value, but some, such as timeshares, will not. There’s a special government department known as the Valuation Office Agency (VAO) which deals with these cases. This is explained in the HMRC manual at IHTM23207.

The basic principle in the valuation of an asset is to determine what would be agreed at arm’s length between a theoretical willing buyer and a willing seller. This has become particularly challenging with timeshares due to the limited market for selling them. It is much easier to buy a timeshare than to sell one. There are usually ongoing costs to be taken into account, and I understand that in some cases HMRC has accepted that they have no value.

As an experienced buyer of timeshares, I suspect you will have been aware of these issues and have chosen carefully so that yours have retained their value. You may also have bought it in the secondary market rather than buying it new.

You say that you bought them in part so that your children could enjoy them. If this is the case, you might consider transferring some of them as lifetime gifts to your adult children with the intention that they fall out of your estate after seven years. If they have increased in value, you will need to consider the capital gains tax position, of course.

In addition, if you also use them after making the transfer, you will need to consider the inheritance tax reservation of benefit rules, which can apply unless you pay your children for the times that you occupy the property. The payment will be considered their income and may be subject to income tax, depending on their individual circumstances. You should keep evidence that you have paid a market rent, which might be available from advertised rates for similar properties.

One of the problems with the reservation of benefit rules is that it is easy to be caught out inadvertently. For example, what happens if you give a property to your adult child but still visit on occasion? Fortunately, the guidance provides some help at IHTM14333, where examples of short visits are allowed.

Nevertheless, in cases like this involving family members, I usually suggest arranging a formal written contract which sets out the terms, including an “adjuster clause”. This essentially states that the parties intend for the terms to be the open market rate for the use of the premises. If it is subsequently agreed with HMRC that a different amount should have applied, the terms will be adjusted to reflect that difference.

In the event of a challenge by HMRC, the clause will take effect, preventing the reservation rules from applying and ensuring no loss of inheritance tax. Consequently, the inspector will have no incentive to pursue it.

I know people who have been very happy with their timeshares. They enjoy taking their holidays in the same place and at the same time each year. They meet up with their neighbours again and still appreciate the attractions that drew them to the area in the first place.

However, many timeshares were sold at high prices using a glossy catalogue and on the basis of being able to exchange your weeks for time at a different resort. This was to be arranged for a fee through the company involved. In practice, it became very difficult for people to take advantage of this when trying to select available slots. It was particularly difficult for those who took out loans at high interest rates to purchase their timeshare.

In addition, our requirements change as we age, of course. You may buy, hoping to enjoy holidays with your children and grandchildren, but they grow up and want to spend their time in other ways. Buying a property to use as a holiday home is an expensive alternative, but you typically have much more flexibility once you decide to sell and move on. However, I recognise that this may be difficult to do in the current economic climate.

Mike Warburton was previously a tax director with accountants Grant Thornton and is now retired. His columns should not be taken as advice, or as a personal recommendation, but as a starting point for readers to undertake their own further research.

data.title: ‘Ridiculous’ brick colour rules holding up housebuilding, warns construction boss
data.description:
“Ridiculous” planning rules on the colour of bricks are causing huge delays to housebuilding, the boss of a major property developer has warned.

Tim Roberts, the chief executive of Henry Boot, said council paperwork required to secure detailed planning consent “when you determine what colour of bricks and roof tiles you use” was adding years of delays to some projects.

He said: “It’s ridiculous – when we can buy a site, build homes and sell them in less than three years – that some sites are taking us three years just to agree what the colour of the brick is.

“Not all local authorities are slow. Some will clear detailed planning in, say, 12 months. But we’ve had situations where we’ve gone to appeal on non-determination of detailed planning consent, which is nonsense because you’re not arguing over principles, you’re arguing over detail.”

It comes as the Government struggles to make significant headway on its manifesto pledge to build 1.5 million homes by the end of this parliament.

Sir Keir Starmer, the Prime Minister, has pinned much of his hopes on planning reform, including setting compulsory housing targets for councils and enabling more construction on low-quality green belt land.

However, housebuilding remains at its lowest level in almost a decade, with energy performance certificates (EPC) granted for new homes falling by 8pc over the 12 months to June 2025.

At the same time, Angela Rayner’s sudden departure as housing secretary has left Steve Reed, her successor, scrambling to get up to speed with his new brief.

Mr Roberts said reforms needed to extend to the detailed planning approvals process if Labour were to have any hope of hitting its housing target. It is at this stage that features such as brick colours are approved by local authorities.

He added that councils needed to crack down on staff working from home, which he claimed was contributing to delays.

Mr Roberts said: “This is about the productivity of the country ... and I’m afraid to say if you look at the long-term productivity of the public sector, it is very poor.

“If you look at the work-from-home policies that the public sector has, it is vastly different from the private sector. You’ve got to have efficiencies and targets so that planning is done more efficiently.”

Henry Boot on Tuesday sold its construction arm in a managed buyout, marking its exit as a contractor following an industry slowdown. While the construction arm was set up as a separate division some 55 years ago, Henry Boot’s roots go back to 1886, and the business was a wartime contractor by 1914.

A Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government spokesman said: “We inherited a broken planning system but took decisive action to overhaul it and it’s pleasing to hear that these changes are having an effect.

“Through our pro-growth reforms, alongside recruiting over 300 planners, we will go even further though and get spades in the ground and cranes in the sky to unleash the biggest era of building in the country’s history.”

data.title: How Burnham used taxpayer cash to fill Manchester with luxury skyscrapers
data.description:
Andy Burnham believes he has a plan to fix Britain’s faltering economy.

The Greater Manchester Mayor has set out a range of policies to “turn the country around”, including higher council tax rates on expensive homes in London and the South East, and an overhaul of income tax to make the rich pay more. It comes as the Labour Mayor prepares what many believe is a leadership challenge to Sir Keir Starmer.

Burnham’s insistence that he can save the economy invites scrutiny of his record in Greater Manchester, where he has been in charge since 2017.

When he was first elected, the Labour Mayor promised to deliver affordable housing and eradicate rough sleeping. However, while building has surged, he has failed to fix homelessness and his record has been dogged by controversy surrounding his dealings with a single property developer: Renaker.

His Greater Manchester Combined Authority (GMCA) has handed the company £800m in loans since 2016 to build in the area.

The money has been used to part-finance a raft of tower blocks that have reshaped Manchester’s skyline, including introducing the tallest building outside London.

Burnham has said Renaker is regenerating the area and bringing growth to Manchester. However, questions have been raised about whether Manchester really needs luxury skyscrapers given many of the flats in them are being bought up by Asian investors.

Burnham’s authority has also faced legal action over claims he unfairly favoured Renaker with the loans, though he successfully defended the case.

Renaker was founded in 2006 by Daren Whitaker, a construction industry veteran who previously worked at Laing O’Rourke. He has said he launched the company “with a vision to build a positive legacy in Manchester city centre and to change the Manchester skyline”.

He has certainly done the latter: the developer has built or proposed a dozen skyscrapers in the city, helping Mr Burnham make progress towards his target to build 75,000 homes by the end of the decade.

However, the towers have done little to alleviate Manchester’s housing problems. It is understood there are no affordable flats in some of the main taxpayer-funded developments completed by Renaker.

This is despite targets set by the local council that require all new developments in Manchester to be made up of at least 20pc affordable homes.

Last year Manchester council approved a £300m, 71-storey Renaker tower in Deansgate with 642 flats, a swimming pool, sauna and top-floor restaurant.

Councillors also granted approval for four neighbouring residential buildings up to 51 storeys high, also being built by Renaker. The towers will provide 1,746 homes as well as a dog spa, mahjong room and podcast studio.

The Telegraph has previously reported that hundreds of flats in two taxpayer-backed skyscrapers built by Renaker have been sold to Asian investors, rather than locals.

Despite all this building, there are signs that the area’s housing crisis is getting worse. Official figures show that in March this year there were 2,075 families living in temporary accommodation in Greater Manchester, up from 1,566 in March 2020.

Meanwhile, 154 people were seen sleeping rough on a single night in Greater Manchester in autumn 2024, up from 149 a year earlier. It comes despite Mr Burnham pledging to eradicate rough sleeping in the city by 2020.

A GMCA spokesman said: “Because of the action we’ve taken, we’ve reduced rough sleeping by 43pc since 2017, against the backdrop of a 1.7pc drop across England in the same period. In 2021 we had brought the number of people sleeping rough down to just 89, from a high of 268 four years earlier.

“Last night 615 people who would otherwise be at risk of rough sleeping were supported in A Bed Every Night accommodation, set up by the Mayor in 2018.”

Last month, Renaker came to an agreement with the Manchester city council to pay £114m towards affordable housing elsewhere in the city, part of a deal to progress its five towers in development.

However, the company may never end up paying the full amount. The council agreed with the company that “viability issues” prevented it from paying the sum at the moment, with officials instead pledging to reassess the feasibility of making such payments at a later date.

Beyond scrutiny of the type of housing delivered, the financial support for Renaker has also been questioned.

John Leech, a Liberal Democrat city councillor, has raised concerns over the lack of transparency surrounding the loans made to Renaker. Last year he called for “more care and attention to such enormous sums of money being handed out by the GMCA.”

In July, Mr Burnham’s administration fended off a lawsuit that claimed officials unfairly loaned taxpayer cash to Renaker.

Rival property developer, Weis Group, sued the authority claiming that Mr Burnham’s officials were “distort[ing]” Manchester’s property market by providing Whitaker with “advantageous treatment”.

The Competition Appeals Tribunal ultimately ruled in favour of GMCA and said the authority had acted fairly.

A GMCA spokesman said: “GMCA won the tribunal case on every count. The chair praised our approach, which helped deliver 11,000 new homes across Greater Manchester, regenerating brownfield sites at no cost to the taxpayer.

“We reinvested our share of the interest in our work to tackle the housing crisis, including launching the country’s first Good Landlord Charter, and training new housing enforcement officers to drive up standards for renters. At the same time, we have used our £175m Brownfield Housing Fund to help deliver 4,000 affordable homes.”

Mr Burnham has repeatedly defended his administration’s decision to hand taxpayer cash to Renaker. Speaking to The Telegraph last year, he said the housing fund that provided the loans was intended to “bring regeneration” to Manchester, rather than deliver affordable homes.

He said: “Renaker, in my view, should be commended for the commitment they’ve made to Manchester. The fund was created because London-based organisations wouldn’t make a commitment to Manchester.

“[Renaker is] building out high-quality infrastructure that the city needs, that has driven our growth. It should be celebrated as a success story, without an implication that there’s something wrong.”

He added that there had “not been a single default” on loans and financial returns from the lending had paid for some social and affordable housing outside of the city centre.

This building boom has helped to spur growth in Manchester. The city’s economy grew by 9.1pc to £110.15m in 2023, according to figures from the ONS. That outpaced growth of 8.4pc in the West Midlands and 8pc in London over the same period.

Yet challenges remain. Unemployment rate has risen from 5.2pc in 2018, the year after Burnham took charge, to 6.2pc last year.

Additionally, the economic inactivity rate for Greater Manchester stood at 25pc in the year to March 2025, above the UK average of 21.6pc.

And despite the frenzy of construction activity, Burnham may yet miss the Government’s housebuilding target for the region. A report published last year by the think tank Centre for Cities warned that Greater Manchester was likely to fall short by more than 23,000 homes.

A GMCA spokesman said: “With the right national support we can build 75,000 new houses by 2030, including 10,000 low carbon homes.

“We have mapped out shovel-ready sites and, backed by government support and private sector investment, can deliver the homes our city region needs.”

Renaker was contacted for comment.

data.title: Japan just days away from running out of Asahi beer
data.description:
Japan risks running out of the country’s most popular beer within days after a cyber attack forced the brewer to halt production.

Shop owners said they were expecting Asahi beers to be out of stock imminently after an outage affected production for the fourth day.

Asahi revealed on Monday that it had been hit by a cyber attack, causing a system failure which left it unable to process orders, ship deliveries and offer customer service in Japan.

It has sparked a crunch in supplies of Asahi Super Dry, Japan’s best-selling beer.

Lawson, one of the country’s largest convenience store owners, told the Financial Times it was “possible that some of these products may become increasingly out of stock from tomorrow onwards”.

Another retail executive told the FT that his stores would “run out of products soon”, adding: “When it comes to Super Dry, I think we’ll run out in two or three days at supermarkets and Asahi’s food products within a week or so.”

It threatens to fuel a wave of panic buying among Japanese drinkers. While Japan’s beer consumption has been shrinking over the past 20 years, the country still consumes around 296.9bn bottles a year, equal to around 54.4 large bottles per person.

Asahi said it sold 73 million cases of Super Dry in Japan in 2024, as well as 12 million cases of its alcohol free Style Free beer and 12 million cases of its Clear Asahi, a US-style lager.

The attack has been limited to Asahi’s Japanese operations. It said none of its manufacturing in Europe – including its UK beer supply – would be impacted by the incident.

The company said it did not know when it would be able to bring its Japanese systems back online but added it was unaware that any customer data had been stolen.

A wave of disruption has been caused by recent cyber attacks across the globe, with both Jaguar Land Rover and Marks & Spencer having fallen victim to major hacks over the past year.

Jaguar Land Rover was last month forced to bring its manufacturing to a standstill after it was hit by a cyber attack on August 31. It only started to bring systems back online this week.

Meanwhile, Marks & Spencer is still reeling from its cyber attack in the spring. The company said earlier this year that it expected a £300m hit to profits from the attack.

Asahi declined to comment on any possible shortages in Japan. Lawson could not immediately be reached for comment.

data.title: Thames Water lenders prepared to take £7bn hit to avoid nationalisation
data.description:
A group of Thames Water creditors is prepared to effectively write off £7.5bn of debt in a bid to avoid nationalisation.

The consortium has proposed a turnaround plan that would cancel around a third of Thames Water’s near-£20bn debt pile and commit the group to investing in the company.

Outstanding fines would be paid – but the investor group, dubbed London & Valley Water, would also seek leniency on future penalties in exchange for investment.

Thames Water revealed over the summer that annual losses had hit £1.6bn as a soaring debt burden and continued sewage failures fuelled its financial crisis.

Bosses said it would take at least a decade to turn around Britain’s largest water company.

The company’s creditors are looking to secure backing for their plans to avoid the utility being put into a temporary special administration regime, which would effectively wipe out their investments.

Lenders said they would not pay a dividend while conducting their turnaround plans or until the company is listed.

They also committed not to sell the business before the end of March 2030.

The consortium said the “more ambitious” plan would increase investment in Thames Water by around £1bn compared to proposals they submitted to Ofwat in May.

The latest proposal was the result of “three months of detailed discussion” with the water regulator and would “support long-term financial resilience and is intended to help the company pay for outstanding fines”, it added.

Mike McTighe, a City veteran whose current roles include chairman of Telegraph Media Group, would become chairman of Thames Water under the creditors’ proposals.

He said: “There is a huge amount of work to be done to turn around Thames Water and deliver the improved service and environmental outcomes that customers and local communities deserve.

“From day one, we will inject billions in new investment, strengthen Thames Water’s balance sheet, transform the company for thousands of hard-working frontline staff and begin the delivery of an operational turnaround that puts 16 million customers and the environment first.”

Lenders of Thames Water’s “Class A” debt would receive at least 10pc of new equity in the utility company in exchange for writing off about 25pc of their stake, worth around £4bn.

Thames Water said it remains in discussions over the bid but any agreement would be subject to High Court approval.

Chris Weston, the chief executive of Thames Water, said: “Today is an important milestone in our ongoing work with creditors and stakeholders to secure a market-led recapitalisation that establishes the financial and regulatory foundations required to support the investment and performance improvements our customers expect.”

data.title: Put aside your ego and you’ll see downsizing gives you freedom
data.description:
In a week of gloom for the property market – Kirstie Allsopp says “it’s dead” and the papers are full of tales of woe from those with expensive houses that have been on the market for years – I offer a differing view.

In January we decided to downsize. All of this year we’ve been trying to sell the house we’ve lived in for 23 years; the family home where we’ve raised our children, sent them to school, seen them take their first steps and now punted them off to university and beyond.

It’s been emotional. First the decision to do it. “You can only sell the family home once,” warned an older friend. She’s right, it’s hard to part with the walls which have witnessed so many happy memories. But I also think it is crazy to stay in a house for the sake of Christmas dinner. So many families keep a huge barn of a place they no longer need just to be able to muster the clan at key moments. Isn’t that what an Airbnb is for?

We decided to downsize to free up some cash. Like many Britons, our family house is currently the custodian of all our financial Lego chips. We’ve almost paid off the mortgage, but we realised that even if we do, all our money will still be in the house (and with the prospect of Rachel Reeves imposing capital gains tax on primary residences, it felt like a good time to sell).

It’s also the right time. We have one child about to finish university and the other out in the world: do we really need a four-bedroom family house? When the children aren’t there we fairly rattle around. It costs a fortune to heat the big, empty rooms, particularly as energy prices have soared. And we’re in the catchment area for several excellent state schools, none of which we need anymore. It’s a brilliant house for raising a family, but that isn’t us anymore. Our children are raised. It’s time to move on, to clear out so that someone else can benefit from the house and its position: the place needs the patter of tiny feet, the rhythm of children going to school and coming home. We bought it when I was four months pregnant with our first child – she’s about to turn 23.

The trouble is that while it makes sense to sell up, to reorganise our finances into different, more accessible piles, it is also hard to do. We’ve spent months on a RightMove binge – usually in bed, both of us clicking and swiping and comparing. We looked around nearly 40 flats, eventually deciding we needed three bedrooms, in case the kids boomerang home; but in their absence the rooms can be an office and a snug. We couldn’t afford three bedrooms and a garden. That is a compromise.

Finding looking at London flats depressing, we considered moving out of the capital. You can buy a palace in the country for the price of a two-bed in London. So we started playing Escape to the Country for real – the space, the garden – until the reality of being hours from family, friends, community, work and culture brought us back to our senses. We’re staying in inner London for now; it’s my happy place. But the countryside fantasy made me realise that much of my reluctance to downsize was wrapped up with status. By moving out we could have a bigger, cheaper house. But the reality of downsizing is exactly that: living somewhere smaller and less swanky. Looking round all those flats, I’ve realised that there is a lot of ego and status involved in where we decide to live. If we’re going effectively to right-size (my preferred term to downsizing, which feels depressing and regressive), we need to get over ourselves and our status anxiety and look to the bigger picture.

The truth is, we don’t need all our current space, and before we lived in this big house we were totally happy in smaller flats. This move is about right-organising our lives now that we are in our mid-50s. That involves swapping real-estate square-footage and our glorious floor-to-ceiling windows for a smaller flat which has enough space for us all but also leaves us mortgage-free with a bit left over. That means freedom; if it all goes through (fingers crossed we are moving next month), my husband can retire and I’ll only have to do the work that excites me. I am aware that is a hugely privileged position to be in.

Taking the ego hit on the house means less stress, more pleasure and leisure and the opportunity to help our fledgling adult children by paying for Masters degrees (increasingly necessary to get them on the jobs ladder) or to act as the bank of Mum and Dad if they want to get on the housing market themselves in due course. In the meantime there is still space for all of us in the new pad.

The last year has been a lesson not just in real estate but in working out what success looks like to us now. It’s been a big shift. I’ve moved from feeling that my nice house which I’ve worked hard to pay for defines me, to realising that success for me now isn’t a status abode but the freedom to live the life I want. I’ve learnt that getting over my real-estate ego equals freedom. That the smaller flat – yes, we finally found something that fit our criteria of having something nice about it and three bedrooms, just around the corner from where we live now but for half the money – means liberation from monthly payments and having to work to meet them.

There’s a compromise; our new flat definitely does not have the wow factor of our old house. It’s on a busier road, it’s not as pretty; Hampstead Heath will have to be our garden.

I am writing this because, as a property columnist for many years, I saw so many people who were trapped, Miss Havisham-like, in houses that no longer fitted them; clinging to the past. Yes, change is difficult, and scary. But taking the plunge, selling up, deciding to define success differently and live in a new way is exciting and rejuvenating.

Change, after all, is evidence of life. It’s not only good for you, but helps the next generation by freeing up family homes near good schools. And yes, we are in the process of a massive clear-out (clothes, books, old toys, the detritus of a quarter of a century), which is hard, but we are getting there. And success for me now is about freedom, not a status house.

So what would right-sizing look like for you? Remember, freedom is success: success is freedom. What are you waiting for?

data.title: The baby-faced union boss making commuters miserable
data.description:
He’s a baby-faced, softly spoken former market trader from Deptford, south-east London, with a penchant for bingo.

Yet for all his disarming traits, Eddie Dempsey is causing misery for millions of workers and tourists in London this week as he leads crippling strikes on the London Underground.

Dempsey, 43, is the head of the National Union of Rail, Maritime and Transport Workers (RMT) and cut from the same cloth as his chip-on-the-shoulder predecessors Mick Lynch and Bob Crow.

While other union bosses often toed the line and kowtowed to the Left, Dempsey has displayed an alarming capacity to push the envelope – even going so far as to praise a noted Russian warlord after Putin’s initial invasion of Ukraine.

During a visit to the country in 2015 following Russia’s incursion into the Donbas region, he was pictured posing with and praising pro-Putin militia leaders.

Dempsey had gone into Ukraine on a “humanitarian” mission but was pictured alongside Aleksey Mozgovoy, the former head of the pro-Russian “Ghost Brigade”.

Mozgovoy, who had sought to ban young women from bars in the territories he controlled while urging them to have children to boost the Russian-speaking population, was killed two weeks after the visit.

Later, the union leader wrote a tribute describing him as a “charismatic” insurgent.

Dempsey, who was elected to the RMT leadership in March, has also previously clashed with the Left over his support for Brexit and the white working classes. He suggested that backers of Tommy Robinson, regardless of their bigotry, were “right to hate” the liberal Left.

He added that some in Labour had calculated that support from more affluent workers, together with ethnic minorities and liberals, would be enough to get into power and that the interests of the majority of workers could be sacrificed.

On Wednesday, with commuters hit by the misery of the Tube strike, Dempsey again chose to provoke the Left, lashing out at Labour and attacking Sir Sadiq Khan for his stance on the dispute.

Addressing the TUC Congress in Brighton on Wednesday, Dempsey condemned the Mayor of London for negotiating via social media and spouting “old, tired clichés”.

His message to Sir Keir Starmer was even more stark. Dempsey warned that the RMT has no plans to affiliate with Labour after it split from the party in 2006 and could even throw its weight behind the party being formed by Jeremy Corbyn.

“We supported him when he was in Labour and we supported him when he was an independent,” he told the PoliticsJoe podcast.

Dempsey said he had no idea and did not care who was running for the Labour deputy leadership and that RMT members “are not that interested”.

He said: “It’s no wonder people are drifting away, if you want people to support you, you’ve got to deal with working class issues. We’re interested in results. It’s transactional. If you are doing a good job for our members we’ll back you, if not we’ll have a row with you.”

One union insider said Dempsey had been “the big hit at the TUC”.

The official said: “Eddie has got 10,000 people out on strike, which helps, but people know him from his double act with Mick Lynch during the last strikes a few years ago. He’s not doing this to be a celebrity, but he has a big profile and it’s going to get bigger.”

Dempsey grew up in the tough environment of the Woodpecker Estate in New Cross and is a supporter of Millwall Football Club – whose fans revel in the chant “no one likes us, we don’t care” – as was Crow, whom Dempsey has cited as a major influence.

Like Lynch and Mick Cash, his two immediate predecessors, he also comes from an Irish Catholic background, which played a formative role in his politics as he was influenced by socialist figures like James Connolly.

Prior to his high-profile role in the RMT, Dempsey was London secretary of the Connolly Association, the oldest migrant workers’ organisation in Britain and named after James Connolly, an Irish republican who was executed during the 1916 Easter Rising.

He said in a 2019 blog post that he had been introduced to Marxism by “veteran comrades” such as Communist Party activist Monty Goldman and Max Levitas, an Irish-born communist campaigner in London’s East End.

Dempsey’s first job was as a trader at Deptford market, where he honed some of the negotiating skills he later brought to bear at the RMT.

He later moved on to the railway, first as a platform worker and then as a train driver, before rising through the ranks of the RMT as it engaged in numerous disputes over pay, perks and attempts to cut jobs.

He also played a major part in the RMT’s campaign to leave the European Union, which the union distrusted over its stance on organised labour and moves to encourage privatisation.

Advancing to assistant general secretary, he accompanied Lynch on media rounds during 2022 and 2023 strikes in which the RMT was perceived to have got the better of Tory ministers and ultimately won a new pay deal.

Dempsey made headlines in March when he said he would celebrate becoming leader of the RMT by playing bingo at his local social club with his wife, and was reported to be living in a council house in Islington, north London, despite a £100,000 pay package.

Dempsey also hit out at Labour MP Clive Lewis, who he claimed had compared him to Sir Oswald Mosley. He said he had grown up in one of the most diverse estates in Britain and that his political heroes had fought fascism and later opposed Enoch Powell.

With Londoners’ goodwill fast evaporating, Dempsey’s Tube strike is a major gamble. He may have left Deptford, but he’s still a trader at heart.

data.title: ‘Save British double-deckers from cheap Chinese imports’
data.description:
China is threatening to steal Britain’s crown as a leader in electric bus manufacturing with an influx of cheap imports, the head of the country’s biggest bus-maker has warned.

Jean-Marc Gales, chief executive of Wrightbus, said Chinese rivals such as BYD and Yutong were receiving huge state subsidies from Beijing, making them a threat to home-grown UK manufacturers unless urgent action was taken by the Government.

Gales said a lower level of subsidy funding from the Government, one that had served to encourage British bus operators and councils to buy from Wrightbus, had all but dried up – leaving the company vulnerable to Chinese state-subsidised competition.

“What we want is a level playing field, not one where competitors from a particular country get better conditions than all of the others,” he said.

“That does not mean protectionism, but it does mean support. We’re one of the only countries that doesn’t support its own industry in terms of buses.”

He pointed to the example of France, which he said had “an inferior” bus industry that continued to thrive thanks to a requirement for 45pc of all components to be European.

“How about a 40pc UK content to protect this industry? But we never do,” he said.

Wrightbus, based in Northern Ireland, is owned by Jo Bamford, son of JCB chairman Lord Bamford.

It was saved from bankruptcy by Bamford in 2019 and has expanded to 2,300 employees from just 49.

Since then, Wrightbus has done what most other automotive firms have found impossible in overtaking Chinese firm BYD to become the world leader in electric double-deckers after slashing costs to within 5pc of its Asian rival.

But UK financial support is fast evaporating. Remaining funding schemes exclude the retrofitting of diesel bus fleets to run on batteries, depriving Wrightbus of sales of its newest technology while ruling out one of the most affordable routes to cutting carbon emissions for local authorities.

Gales, 62, said the imbalance threatened to limit growth at Wrightbus and opened the way for China to dominate in zero-emission buses just as it does in electric cars.

He said continued success cannot be guaranteed without some measure of government support and incentives.

Wrightbus has repeatedly pressed the case in meetings with Simon Lightwood, the buses minister, he said.

“We talk with them all the time. They listen and they acknowledge, but so far nothing more. We hope that very soon there is another programme because we are ready,” he added.

‘How long can we win?’

Gales, an automotive veteran who led the turnaround of Lotus and was previously second-in-command at Peugeot-Citroen, said the success of Wrightbus since its rescue from insolvency meant it had the potential to become a rare UK export success story.

Annual revenues are expected to reach £600m this year from deliveries of 1,180 buses, 90pc of them electric. This is more than three times the level in 2022, the year before Gales became boss, making it the fastest-growing bus manufacturer in Europe.

Bamford has set a revenue target of £1bn, something the chief executive said could be achieved by reducing the time it took to build a bus by a third while stepping up overseas sales.

However, Gales said that Wrightbus, which is based in Ballymena, County Antrim, needed a strong home market to back up export drives in countries including Singapore, Hong Kong and Australia.

He said: “We can become a world champion for Britain in electric buses. We’ve shown with double-deckers that we can do it. BYD are a giant and we have taken that crown.

“We have the same inventory levels that we had two years ago, but we are building three times as many buses. We use aluminium when the Chinese use steel, our reliability is higher, we have far better support networks and Northern Ireland is competitive on pay.

“But if people are going to take you seriously as an exporter you have to be number one at home. And if there is unfair competition, how long can we win?”

Chinese firms already benefit from cheaper labour and huge economies of scale – the market requires 100,000 new buses a year compared with just 2,000 in the UK – even before the impact of state subsidies is taken into account.

Moreover, China still builds many diesel buses and coaches for domestic use, doing nothing to reduce carbon emissions, while electric models are directed at markets such as the UK.

The British city-bus sector is already dominated by electric vehicles (EVs), which account for around 70pc of annual sales, a far greater proportion than for cars, where penetration stands at just 20pc.

Wrightbus controls slightly more than one third of the overall UK new bus market, with seven or eight other manufacturers active, including Chinese firms with about 20pc. Yet increasing that share significantly might not be possible, said Gales.

In response to that reality Wrightbus has developed its conversion division, which allows a diesel bus halfway through its working life of some 18 years to become zero-emission for around £200,000 – or half the price of a new EV bus.

Lorries of the future

In a further development of the repowering model, Wrightbus last week unveiled its first electric truck conversion, costing around £115,000.

The brainchild of what Gales called “some of our Freds in the shed”, the plan involves the conversion of 18-tonne lorries weighing the same as double-decker buses and therefore able to use the exact same drivetrain.

Wrightbus launched the business using a DAF model of which there are 30,000 in operation in the UK, representing a potential conversion market almost as big as the country’s 40,000-strong bus fleet. Just 1pc of trucks in Britain are currently battery-powered.

Gales, who also worked for Fiat and ran the Williams Advanced Engineering, an offshoot from the Formula 1 team, said that in the longer term many buses and trucks might move to hydrogen propulsion.

That will be music to the ears of Bamford, one of the world’s leading advocates for hydrogen and founder of Ryze Power and the Hycap hydrogen investment fund.

Fuel-cell technology remains expensive, Gales said, and is better suited to routes that are longer and can potentially command premium fares, such as a Brighton to Gatwick service for which Wrightbus recently won orders.

However, he added that hydrogen combustion could have far greater potential, offering a straightforward alternative to diesel with a range significantly greater than can be achieved with batteries.

That would allow it to power long-distance coach services and HGVs operating the length of Britain and beyond without the inconvenience and financial and time penalties of having to stop to charge up.

He said: “You need modifications like a new cylinder head because the hydrogen is volatile and burns faster. And you need more sensors and electrics to control the burn, and space in the bus for the tanks.

“But that is something where the UK can also become a world leader – with the right support.”

data.title: AstraZeneca stokes fears of London exit with New York listing
data.description:
AstraZeneca has announced plans to list shares on the New York Stock Exchange, stoking fears that Britain’s biggest drugmaker could quit London.

In a shake-up announced on Monday, the FTSE pharmaceuticals giant said it would upgrade its US listing to ensure it is equally as important in New York as it is on the London Stock Exchange.

Bosses said the move would make it easier for the company to raise money from US investors, describing it as “a global listing for global investors in a global company”.

As part of the announcement, the company said it would maintain its London listing and remain headquartered in the UK.

However, this move will stoke fears over AstraZeneca’s future in London. Analysts at AJ Bell said it hinted “at the possibility of a more dramatic shift at some point in the future”.

Mark Kelly, the chief executive of corporate advisory firm MKP Advisors, said he expected AstraZeneca would leave the London market within the next two or three years, adding: “This is a halfway house, it’s potentially a backdoor route to an eventual full US listing.

“I don’t think anyone is necessarily surprised to see AstraZeneca going this way.”

Ketan Patel, a fund manager at AstraZeneca shareholder Whitefriars, said the risk of the company quitting London was a “concern for the UK market”. He said its exit would come as a “huge blow” to UK investors.

Such concerns will no doubt sound alarm bells for the Labour Government, which is currently trying to rally support among businesses at its party conference in Liverpool.

AstraZeneca, which was the UK’s most valuable listed company up until last week, has long been viewed as one of the businesses most likely to ditch its London listing.

Earlier this year, reports suggested that Sir Pascal Soriot, AstraZeneca’s chief executive, was keen to move the company’s main listing to New York – owing to growing frustration with the UK.

Sir Pascal described the drugmaker as a “very American company”.

The business was formed in 1999 through the merger of Sweden’s Astra AB and Britain’s Zeneca PLC. It remains headquartered in Cambridge.

Mr Kelly said: “AstraZeneca is only really here because when they did the original AstraZeneca merger, it made most sense at the time – London was a bastion of financial equity markets, and it was a wonderful place for a massive company to be listed. It’s difficult to make that argument in 2025.”

Mr Patel said: “There is a clear strategy towards a business that is US-focused, and I would not be surprised if a full exit from London is on the cards. The company already reports and pays dividends in US dollars.”

Currently, more than 40pc of AstraZeneca’s revenue comes from the US, making it the company’s largest market by a significant margin. China accounts for 13pc of revenues, while Europe stands at 21pc.

In recent months, AstraZeneca has been stepping up investment in the US – including a $50bn (£37bn) announcement – following demands from Donald Trump, the US president, for companies to invest more there.

At the same time, the pharmaceutical company has pulled a series of investments from Britain amid an ongoing row with ministers over high medicine charges.

Earlier this month, AstraZeneca halted a £200m investment in its Cambridge research facility after the Government walked away from talks to lower drug charges for medicines in the NHS.

This has fuelled claims that Britain is becoming “uninvestable” for drugmakers.

The Cambridge move followed an earlier decision by AstraZeneca to walk away from a £450m investment in a Liverpool vaccine factory in protest over the Government reducing funding for the site.

Sean Conroy, an analyst at Shore Capital, said the latest move from AstraZeneca could arguably be “a lever and a negotiation tactic to be able to demonstrate that it is cosying up to the US a bit more” – putting Britain under pressure at the same time.

data.title: Passengers face a decade of delays on London-Scotland rail route
data.description:
Millions of passengers face diversions of two hours or more during a decade of engineering work on Britain’s busiest intercity railway line.

A project to renew 1970s-era overhead wires and signalling equipment on the London to Glasgow route will commence in August next year, Network Rail said.

The work will bring years of disruption to 200 miles of the West Coast Main Line (WCML) north of Crewe as sections of track are closed for weeks at a time.

An indication of the likely impact was provided on Monday when train operator Avanti West Coast said the replacement of a bridge on the line will lead to two and a half-hour detours between Preston to Carlisle for two weeks in January.

The work on the Clifton Bridge, which spans the M6 in Cumbria, will see trains diverted off the 125mph main line onto the remote and non-electrified Settle and Carlisle route, which normally has just eight local passenger trains a day in each direction.

It will stretch the 80-mile journey from Preston to Carlisle to one of almost 120 miles, adding 80 minutes to the usual travel time, excluding a longer wait at either end as the service is expected to run only every two hours.

Avanti said it will use the detour to prepare for the years of regular closures beginning later in 2026.

Avanti, which is due to be nationalised in October 2026, said the re-routing will depend on the areas of the WCML that are closed, as well as factors such as the time of year and anticipated passenger loads.

It said the work will deliver a “once-in-a-generation” modernisation that will drive up train performance and boost economic growth.

Phil James, Network Rail’s route director for the North West, said the diversions will allow more people to complete their journeys without transferring to cars and buses, though rail replacement buses will still be required to reach Lancaster and two stations in Cumbria.

“We work together as a rail industry to keep passengers on trains and limit disruption as much as possible during our planned engineering upgrades,” he said.

January’s work will see a 60-year-old bridge over which only one train can pass at a time replaced with a 500-foot, 3,000-tonne structure costing £60m.

In a taste of things to come, the company said people travelling from north-west England will have to choose between making the detour or taking a slow train across the Pennines to reach Scotland by way of the East Coast Mine Line.

Passengers wanting to travel to Glasgow from London will be advised to abandon the WCML completely and use the East Coast line throughout, extending the trip to almost five hours, as will those from Birmingham, via a lengthy journey across the Midlands.

While Avanti runs the most trains on the WCML, carrying 35 million passengers in the year through March, the northern reaches of the route are also used by operators including Northern, TransPennine, ScotRail, Transport for Wales and the Caledonian Sleeper.
