data.title: Hit drivers with pay-per-mile tax to fix Budget, Reeves urged
data.description:
Drivers should be taxed for every mile they travel to help Rachel Reeves fix a hole in Britain’s public finances, a leading think tank has urged.

The Resolution Foundation, which has considerable influence in Labour circles, said vehicle taxes should be overhauled to replace lost fuel duty as more drivers switch from petrol and diesel cars to electric vehicles (EVs).

The think tank has proposed a system to hit drivers with an annual tax plus another fee of between 3p and 9p per mile driven. Drivers of heavier vehicles could be charged the higher price, reflecting wear and tear on Britain’s roads.

“Motoring taxes are an important part of the tax system but they are also an obvious and significant fiscal risk,” said Adam Corlett, who wrote the report.

It’s feared the Chancellor will have to raise as much as £30bn in taxes in her November Budget.

Taxing drivers per mile could raise up to £20bn per year for the Government, the Resolution Foundation suggested.

Fuel duty revenues are set to fall by one fifth by the end of this decade compared to five years ago, from £28bn to £22.6bn, according to the Office for Budget Responsibility.

That drop comes as drivers switch to EVs as well as to hybrid cars and more efficient traditional petrol and diesel models.

Mr Corlett suggested the lightest cars be charged 3p, rising to 9p per mile for heavier models.

“Distance could be logged via a mixture of MOTs, self-reporting and ideally telematics (with the latter helping to exclude miles driven outside the UK and perhaps facilitating local per-mile additions in future to tackle the worst congestion),” his report said.

He also proposes cutting VAT on public chargers from 20pc to 5pc to match the tax on home electricity bills as well as increasing taxes on non-electric cars.

The Resolution Foundation estimates the changes would raise £2bn per year by the end of the decade, £10bn in 2035 and £20bn per year by 2040.

Motoring groups oppose road pricing. Ian Taylor, from the Alliance of British Drivers, said pay per mile schemes are not “particularly fair”.

“It would almost certainly put prices up and probably the only way to administer it is to track everyone, which has freedom and privacy implications,” he said, noting options include roadside cameras or satellites.

“It costs a lot of money to administer, regardless of how you do it.”

Mr Corlett also proposed ending the freeze on fuel duty, which has not increased since 2011. He said the tax should rise by 3pc every year.

Similarly the temporary 5p cut, which is due to expire in March, could be reversed gradually over a number of years to ease the impact “when concern about inflation is likely to still be high”.

This change would see the tax rise from 52.95p per litre currently to almost 70p by the end of the decade.

Petrol cost an average of 134p per litre last week, according to the Department for Transport. That is up by just over 6pc from around 126p before the pandemic – a noticeable increase for drivers.

A Treasury spokesman said: “The Chancellor makes tax policy decisions at fiscal events. We do not comment on speculation around future changes to tax policy.”

data.title: Ryanair accused of ‘disgraceful’ attitude to older passengers
data.description:
Ryanair has been accused of ignoring elderly passengers after confirming plans to scrap paper boarding passes.

A Ryanair shake-up, due to take effect in November, will result in 40 million Ryanair customers who use paper tickets being forced to carry passes on a smartphone, which campaigners said will make travelling harder for older passengers.

Silver Voices, an over-60s lobby group, branded the decision a “disgraceful move” and said the low-cost airline was putting profit ahead of the well-being of customers.

Age UK said it will disadvantage the four million Britons who have never used the internet and the millions more who lack an Android or Apple mobile phone.

On Wednesday, Ryanair said it will adopt digital-only ticketing from Nov 12, meaning “passengers will no longer be able to download and print a physical paper boarding pass”.

It said that travellers “will instead need to use the digital boarding pass generated in their ‘myRyanair’ app during check-in to board their Ryanair flight”.

Ryanair, led by Michael O’Leary, will become the first major European airline to ban paper passes, which are still accepted at British Airways, easyJet, Jet2, Wizz Air and TUI.

Impact on customers

Dennis Reed, director of Silver Voices, accused Ryanair of seeking to “cut costs and cut corners without caring about the impact on their customers”.

He said: “It’s a disgraceful move. They are effectively saying they don’t want older people as passengers. There’s a strong argument to say that it’s discriminatory.”

He argued that many older people do not have smartphones, while those who do may not know how to download an app or have the physical capacity to manipulate a small device, whether due to eyesight issues, arthritis or some form of disability.

He added: “This is going to cause chaos. If somebody goes to the airport to catch a Ryanair flight without the app, what are they going to do? Turn them away?

“It’s also going to isolate older people from society, which is what businesses that go completely digital do.

“I really would urge them to reconsider. I, for one, will not be flying with Ryanair, and I would urge other people to take the same action.”

Caroline Abrahams, charity director at Age UK, said companies should not ignore those with limited digital skills when updating their ticketing processes.

She said: “There should always be an alternative way of booking and showing tickets that does not disadvantage those who aren’t online.”

Ryanair originally planned to scrap paper boarding passes over the summer but shelved the idea amid concerns that it would create confusion and delays at the busiest time for travel.

The airline later said the change would take effect on Nov 3, but has pushed it back by a further nine days to avoid introducing it during the busy half-term school break.

Dara Brady, Ryanair’s chief marketing officer, said the new timing should “ensure a seamless transition” to 100pc digital boarding passes, which he said would mean “a faster, smarter and greener travel experience for our customers”.

Ryanair said the switch should also eliminate almost all airport check-in fees, as passengers will need to check in online or via the app to generate their digital pass.

It said that almost 80pc of its 200 million passengers already use digital boarding passes, and that going paper-free will save over 300 tonnes in waste each year.

The airline claimed that for passengers with impaired vision, the app has the advantage of gathering information in one place, with screen readers able to read the details aloud – something it said “cannot be completed with outdated paper boarding passes”.

It said passengers who have checked in and downloaded a boarding pass will be assisted free of charge at the airport if their phone subsequently dies or is lost.

data.title: Northern Powerhouse Rail to be delayed again in blow to Starmer
data.description:
Northern Powerhouse Rail is to be delayed again, in a blow to Sir Keir Starmer.

Concerns over the cost of the plan to build a rail line between Liverpool and Manchester have led it to be dropped from the Prime Minister’s conference speech.

It had been set to be the flagship announcement at Labour’s annual gathering in Liverpool next week.

Sources told the BBC, which first reported the delay, that it was an attempt to avoid similar mistakes to those made by HS2, which has repeatedly gone over budget.

Officials were said to be particularly concerned about whether connecting Manchester airport to the city centre, a part of Northern Powerhouse Rail that overlapped with part of the cancelled HS2 phase 2, still represented value for money.

Sir Keir had been hoping to use his conference speech to relaunch his premiership after a torrid summer.

He has lost Angela Rayner, former deputy prime minister, after a Telegraph investigation into her tax affairs, while Lord Mandelson, his ambassador to the US, was forced to resign over his links to Jeffrey Epstein.

Morgan McSweeney, his most trusted adviser, is also facing questions over late payments from a think tank he helped set up to the Electoral Commission.

Andy Burnham, whom some Labour MPs are urging to challenge Sir Keir for the Labour leadership, has long been a champion of the project in his job as Manchester mayor.

In response to the plans being delayed again, a source close to Burnham told the BBC: “Any more tyre kicking and there will be no tyre left.”

Northern Powerhouse Rail was initially floated by George Osborne in 2014 as a means of rebalancing the economy, and was projected to cost around £43bn.

Initial plans would have seen the line stretching between Newcastle, Hull, Sheffield and Liverpool, linking to the since-cancelled eastern spur of HS2.

Revisions by Jeremy Hunt in 2022 saw the plans cut back to a new, £17bn line between Liverpool and Manchester.

In June, Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, said she would “set out” plans to “take forward our ambitions for Northern Powerhouse Rail… in the coming weeks”.

Since the spending review, a black hole of up to £50bn has emerged in the public finances, which she is expected to fill with a swathe of tax rises.

The Labour-linked Resolution Foundation think tank urged the Chancellor earlier this week to cut National Insurance by 2p and add it to income tax.

Another suggestion from the Resolution Foundation, whose former chief executive Torsten Bell is now a Treasury minister after being selected for a safe Labour seat in 2024, is a pay-per-mile tax on motorists.

A Conservative spokesman said: “Yet again it’s more big talk, and no real plans from this Labour Government.

“By bowing down to their union paymasters with inflation-busting pay rises Labour sent the signal that their donors come first, and passengers second – and the fiasco of nationalisation has been proof of that.”

Paul Kohler, the Liberal Democrats’ transport spokesman, said: “This setback delays the vital boost to northern communities that they have been promised.

“The Government must move quickly to resolve these issues, stop the delays and get on with the job. At the moment, this project is just as plagued by delays as actual trains.”

A government source said ministers remain “fully committed to Northern Powerhouse Rail”.

“We are determined to learn from the mistakes of the past and not repeat the previous government’s failures over HS2 and the botched Network North plan.

“That’s why we’re taking the time to get this right. We will set out our plans in the coming weeks”, they said.

data.title: Selfridges owner suffers £130m sales drop as wealthy foreigners avoid London
data.description:
The owner of Selfridges has warned of a slowdown in wealthy foreigners visiting London to shop after suffering a £130m sales hit.

Cambridge Retail Group (CRG), which controls the British luxury department store, said “reduced numbers of international visitors coming to the UK and shopping” led to revenues tumbling last year to £1.44bn versus £1.57bn a year earlier.

The warning will fuel concerns over Britain’s luxury sector after it was dealt a blow from a government decision to axe tax-free shopping for tourists.

Since the move was taken by Rishi Sunak, the then chancellor, wealthy visitors have held back from spending in Britain, instead travelling to the Continent for luxury shopping.

Earlier this year, Lisa Nandy, the culture secretary, said she had heard calls to bring back VAT shopping from companies “very loud and clear”, adding that Labour would be “happy to look at the evidence”.

However, luxury bosses have privately signalled they are gloomy over a change in policy at a time when the Chancellor is under pressure to fill a hole in the public finances of between £20bn and £50bn.

Challenges for CRG

CRG is jointly owned by Saudi Arabia’s Public Investment Fund (PIF) and Thailand’s Central Group, a family-owned retail conglomerate.

In its latest accounts, the company said it could weather the downturn in the UK because it owns other retail brands, including De Bijenkorf in the Netherlands, as well as Brown Thomas and Arnotts in Ireland.

Gross profits were down around £50m to £792m, with the group pointing to a “challenging retail and consumer environment”. At Selfridges itself, sales fell 7pc to £775m.

It also changed its financial year, which it said was partly to blame for weaker sales, given the latest accounts were for a slightly shorter period.

The accounts mark the first set under Cambridge Retail Group’s new ownership.

PIF last October struck a deal to become a junior partner in Selfridges, buying out Rene Benko’s property business Signa after his empire collapsed.

It followed months of turmoil for the group, after a fraud investigation was launched into Mr Benko. He has since been accused of hiding watches and other valuables so they could not be used to pay creditors. 

Mr Benko has denied any wrongdoing.

data.title: David Beckham lands £25m payday
data.description:
Sir David Beckham is in line for a $34m (£25m) payout after advertising tie-ups with the likes of Stella Artois and Hugo Boss delivered a record year for his brand.

DRJB Holdings, the holding company that oversees the former footballer’s separate brand and TV production business, paid dividends of $75.7m to shareholders including Sir David, according to newly-filed accounts.

The former Manchester United star, who was knighted this year for services to sport and charity, is entitled to 45pc of the payout through his company Footwork Productions, which owns a minority stake in DRJB Holdings.

The remaining 55pc goes to US sports branding giant Authentic Brands, which bought a portion of DRJB Holdings for $269m three years ago. It will also receive a separate $5.6m dividend because it owns preference shares.

The dividend is an increase from the total $67.4m paid out last year.

The latest figures show the former England captain is continuing to cash in from his personal brand even after ceding control of the business.

Through brand management arm DB Ventures, which is owned by DRJB Holdings, Sir David has struck lucrative marketing deals with brands including SharkNinja and Stella.

He is expected to sign further sponsorship deals in the run-up to the 2026 Fifa World Cup – following a partnership with US telecoms group Verizon.

The 50-year-old also founded Inter Miami FC and has more than 160 million followers on social media.

DRJB Holdings controls Studio 99, the production business behind the 2023 Beckham documentary series for Netflix. Another documentary about his wife Victoria, the fashion designer and former Spice Girl, is due to be released on Netflix next week.

DRJB Holdings reported a pre-tax profit of $45m last year – an increase of almost a quarter. Revenues rose 1pc to just over $92m.

The company said the David Beckham brand had grown “consistently” since the investment from Authentic Brands.

It added: “2024 saw double-digit profit growth as the brand continued to thrive, securing partnerships with world-leading brands whilst entering into innovative new business ventures. The group’s growth momentum has continued into 2025.”

The figures suggest Sir David’s business interests have been unaffected by a high-profile feud within his family.

Eldest son Brooklyn, 26, and his wife Nicola Peltz, an actress and daughter of billionaire activist investor Nelson Peltz, have failed to attend a number of family events this year, including Sir David’s 50th birthday celebrations.

Authentic Brands, which is led by Canadian billionaire Jamie Salter, is home to retail brands including Reebok, Ted Baker and Forever 21.

It also owns the rights to the brand and likeness of individuals including Muhammed Ali and Marilyn Monroe.

The company has embarked on an aggressive acquisition spree in recent years and has been exploring a potential stock market float.

data.title: A ‘taxi tax’ will only hurt the vulnerable. Labour has truly lost the plot
data.description:
“Policies like the taxi tax,” Charlie Gilkes tells me, “destroy the ecosystem in which the hospitality sector functions”.

Mr Gilkes, the co-founder of the Inception Group which owns bars and restaurants across London, explains why it isn’t just the taxi industry which is on high alert following rumours that VAT will be added to all cab journeys.

Hospitality was dealt a devastating blow by the Chancellor’s Budget last year, particularly with the change in employers’ National Insurance contributions. In the nine months that followed the industry lost 89,000 jobs, according to the trade body UKHospitality, which is nearly half of all job losses seen in the wider economy.

A 1.2pc rise in NICs means that the earnings threshold at which employers start to be charged dropped from £9,100 to £5,000 a year, bringing even very low-paid part-time employees into the remit of this tax for the first time.

It is not difficult to see why this policy has hit the industry, which is “a leading creator of part-time jobs, first jobs for young people, older people and people returning to the jobs market”, especially hard.

Against what is already a very challenging backdrop, a taxi tax would hit hospitality businesses even harder, right across the nation. Country publicans are concerned about higher taxi fares putting off patrons. Staff members, particularly women, often rely on taxi shares in the absence of night buses. These workers will face a rise in the cost of their journey home after a late shift.

A large proportion of those who use taxis are vulnerable people, Layla Barke-Jones, a partner at Aaron and Partners Solicitors tells me. They could be the elderly who are unable to drive themselves or those with a disability who find it difficult to use public transport. “These are the people who would be disproportionately hit,” Ms Barke-Jones warns.

Does this mean local authorities – who are increasingly facing scrutiny for their use of private taxis for residents or even asylum seekers – would have to pay the taxi tax too, passing on an additional burden on to taxpayers?

According to Jonathan Main, a VAT expert and partner at professional services firm MHA, the answer to that question, thankfully, is no. Local authorities belong to a separate VAT regime, which allows them to recover any VAT costs incurred in the process of carrying out their statutory duties.

However, if the taxi tax pushes up the cost of cab journeys, not just by the 20pc levied in VAT, but considerably more than that as operators and taxi drivers grapple with the resulting administrative costs, then it’s difficult to see how that extra cost would not be passed on to all consumers.

And that brings us to the group whose livelihoods would be the most directly threatened by this rumoured tax: taxi drivers themselves.

A spokesman for Bolt, the taxi operating service, says: “They are self-employed entrepreneurs, and around 70pc work outside London. A flat 20pc VAT would inevitably raise costs for consumers and reduce demand, leading to a drop in driver earnings.

“We believe VAT should be applied fairly on operators’ margins [as is already the case], not on the full fare, to protect drivers’ livelihoods and keep rides affordable for passengers.”

It is clear that the Budget in late November will be the frostiest yet. A ruinous taxi tax will only prolong the winter.
